proposals.title: Machine Learning for the Inverse Design of Diradical Spin-Photon Interfaces: Mapping Low-Yield Emitters to Quantum Light-Matter Coupling
proposals.motivation: Organic diradicals offer a rich platform for quantum information science due to their high-spin ground states and long-lived quartet excitons, as demonstrated in recent work on luminescent radicals. However, their typically low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is a major obstacle for optoelectronic applications. This weakness could be transformed into a strength for quantum technologies, such as spin-photon interfaces or protected qubits, where strong, coherent light-matter coupling is required but high radiative efficiency is not. A systematic framework to identify and design diradicals with optimal spin-photon coupling properties is currently lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model, trained on first-principles excited-state calculations, can decode the complex relationship between diradical molecular structure and spin-photon coupling efficiency, enabling the inverse design of materials where low PLQY correlates with high performance as a quantum light-matter interface.
proposals.method: We will generate a dataset of diradical structures and compute key properties—spin-orbit coupling matrix elements, transition dipole moments, exchange integrals (J), and quartet state lifetimes—using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) and wavefunction-based methods. This data will train a graph neural network (GNN) to predict spin-photon coupling metrics directly from molecular graphs. The model will be used for high-throughput virtual screening of a large chemical space (e.g., derived from known biradical scaffolds), with active learning guiding new calculations to refine predictions for promising candidates identified by the model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first ML-driven design principle for organic quantum materials based on spin-photon coupling, moving beyond the paradigm of maximizing PLQY. It will deliver a curated library of predicted high-performance diradical structures for experimental synthesis and characterization, directly advancing the field of organic spin-based quantum technologies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit targeting of low-PLQY states as a resource and in applying ML to the specific, quantum-relevant property of spin-photon coupling, rather than conventional optoelectronic metrics. It directly builds upon recent discoveries of long-lived quartet states in radicals to create a predictive design tool.

proposals.title: Optically Addressable High-Spin Networks: Engineering Singlet Pathways for Spin-State Exciton Transport in Polyradical Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Organic high-spin systems, such as triplet ground state diradicals and quartet states in luminescent radicals, offer a promising platform for quantum materials and spintronics. However, controlling and optically addressing individual spins within a magnetically ordered bulk material remains a significant challenge. This project aims to create designer quantum magnets where optical excitations can selectively manipulate spin order, bridging the gap between organic spintronics and quantum information science.
proposals.hypothesis: By deliberately embedding local singlet-stabilizing motifs within a polyradical framework that has a global preference for high-spin (triplet/quartet) interactions, we can create spatially defined 'singlet pathways' that support the formation and directional propagation of spin-state excitons, enabling optical readout and control of the magnetic lattice.
proposals.method: We will synthesize extended 1D and 2D networks using high-spin radical building blocks (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl radical derivatives) coupled via strong ferromagnetic linkers. Singlet wells will be engineered by incorporating diamagnetic spacer units or antiferromagnetically coupled dimers at specific lattice sites. The magnetic and photophysical properties will be characterized by SQUID magnetometry, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy. The dynamics of spin excitons will be modeled using exact diagonalization and density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) methods to map the emergent singlet pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for creating optically active quantum magnets, demonstrating the first observation of engineered spin-state exciton transport in an organic polyradical solid. It will provide a foundational material platform for testing concepts in spin-based quantum sensing and information transfer.
proposals.novelty: While high-spin radicals and their photophysics are known, the intentional topological engineering of a material to have competing singlet and high-spin interactions to guide spin excitons is a novel and non-obvious strategy for creating an optically addressable spin lattice.

proposals.title: Spin-Selective Photophysics in Diradicals with Inverted Singlet-Triplet Energy Ordering
proposals.motivation: In organic semiconductors, the typical energy ordering (S1 > T1) leads to spin-forbidden reverse intersystem crossing (RISC), limiting efficiency. Recent work on luminescent radicals has demonstrated efficient doublet emission and long-lived quartet states, but the fundamental photophysics of diradicals where the singlet is *lower* in energy than the triplet (E_S < E_T) remains unexplored. This inverted regime could unlock new spin-dependent pathways for light emission and charge separation, moving beyond the constraints of conventional Hund's rule.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in carefully designed organic diradicals, a ground-state singlet (S0) to excited singlet (S1) energy lower than the triplet (T1) can be achieved, and that spin-selective intersystem crossing (ISC) from this low-energy S1 to a higher-energy, long-lived quartet state (Q1) will provide a novel radiative decay channel.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations to screen candidate diradical architectures (e.g., based on triangulene derivatives or strongly coupled nitroxide-biradicals) for E_S < E_T. Promising candidates will be synthesized and characterized via transient electron paramagnetic resonance (trEPR) and ultrafast optical spectroscopy to directly probe the quartet state formation and dynamics. Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and electroluminescence in simple device architectures will be measured to assess the viability of the S1→Q1→S0 radiative pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first design principles for organic diradicals with inverted singlet-triplet ordering and map the resulting spin-dependent photophysics. It will contribute a new material class to the field of radical-based optoelectronics, potentially leading to emitters that circumvent efficiency limits imposed by triplet states. The exploration of quartet-mediated emission will expand the understanding of multi-spin excited states in organic materials.
proposals.novelty: While the Friend group has pioneered the use of doublet and quartet states in radicals, all prior work assumes E_S > E_T. This proposal deliberately inverts this fundamental energetic relationship to explore a new regime of spin dynamics, which is non-obvious and unexplored in the context of luminescent radicals and diradicals.

proposals.title: Intrinsic Singlet-Triplet Manifolds and Magnetic Anisotropy of Isolated Molecular Diradicals in Rigid Matrices
proposals.motivation: Molecular diradicals are promising for quantum information and spintronics due to their multi-spin states. However, their fundamental electronic and magnetic properties are often obscured by intermolecular interactions in films or aggregates. Establishing a pristine spectroscopic baseline for an isolated diradical is a critical, missing prerequisite for rationally designing materials where these interactions are deliberately engineered, as in recent work on quartet-state charge transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by isolating diradicals in inert, rigid matrices at cryogenic temperatures, we can directly measure their intrinsic singlet-triplet energy gap, zero-field splitting (ZFS), and magnetic anisotropy, which are unperturbed by intermolecular coupling or molecular motion.
proposals.method: We will dope carefully selected, stable organic diradicals (e.g., Chichibabin's hydrocarbon derivatives or triangulene-type structures) at ultralow concentrations (<0.01 mol%) into inert, rigid host matrices (e.g., crystalline biphenyl or polystyrene glass). Samples will be studied at liquid helium temperatures (1.8-10 K). High-frequency/field EPR (e.g., 120-240 GHz) will precisely determine the ZFS tensors and g-anisotropy. SQUID magnetometry will measure the temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibility to extract the singlet-triplet energy gap (ΔEST). Complementary optical (absorption, photoluminescence) and transient absorption spectroscopy will probe the electronic structure and excited-state dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, high-precision dataset defining the intrinsic ground and excited state properties of prototypical diradicals. It will establish a critical reference for future studies on diradical films and aggregates, enabling the quantification of intermolecular exchange and charge transfer effects. This baseline is essential for advancing the design of radical-based OLEDs and quantum magnets.
proposals.novelty: While diradicals have been studied in solids, systematic isolation to extract purely intramolecular parameters, free from ensemble averaging and intermolecular perturbations, represents a novel and necessary foundational step. The combined use of high-frequency EPR and SQUID on identical, ultra-dilute samples will yield unprecedented detail on the isolated spin Hamiltonian.

proposals.title: Molecular Engineering of Strongly Exchange-Coupled Diradicals for Thermally Stable Quartet State Populations in Organic Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that luminescent organic radicals, particularly those with long-lived quartet states, enable intermolecular charge separation and novel light-emitting mechanisms. However, the thermal stability of the spin-polarized populations (e.g., quartet vs. doublet) is governed by the ground-state exchange energy (J), which is often small in reported systems, limiting device performance at room temperature. Increasing |J| is crucial for stabilizing desired spin configurations against thermal mixing, a key step towards practical radical-based optoelectronics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically designing diradical molecular cores with enhanced through-bond coupling and reduced conformational flexibility, we can significantly increase the intramolecular exchange energy, leading to improved thermal stability of the quartet ground state population.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) and multireference calculations (e.g., CASSCF/NEVPT2) to screen and optimize novel diradical designs based on known chromophores like perchlorotriphenylmethyl. Specific design strategies include: 1) Fusing conjugated bridges (e.g., acenes, thiophenes) between radical centers to enhance superexchange, 2) Incorporating steric locks to enforce coplanarity and maximize orbital overlap, and 3) Tuning the linker's electronic structure (quinoid vs. aromatic) to control the singlet-triplet gap. Promising candidates will be synthesized and characterized via variable-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and magnetometry to directly measure J.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear molecular design rules for achieving large exchange couplings in open-shell organic semiconductors. It will deliver new high-J diradical materials with characterized thermal spin stability, providing a fundamental materials platform for the next generation of quantum light-emitting diodes and spin-based organic devices.
proposals.novelty: While previous work focused on photophysical properties of isolated radicals, this proposal systematically targets the ground-state exchange Hamiltonian itself. The explicit design for large J, rather than just luminescence efficiency, is a distinct and necessary step for engineering robust spin-based functionality.

proposals.title: Correlating Engineered Diradical Character with High-Temperature Spin Coherence in Molecular Qubits
proposals.motivation: Molecular spin qubits offer unparalleled chemical tunability for quantum technologies, but their quantum coherence is typically limited to cryogenic temperatures. Recent work has shown that organic radicals and diradicaloids possess long-lived spin states, yet a systematic understanding of how ground-state electronic structure dictates thermal resilience is lacking. Establishing a direct link between a quantifiable molecular property—diradical character—and high-temperature coherence would unlock a materials-by-design pathway for practical quantum information science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a molecule's degree of incipient diradical character, a ground-state electronic property, directly governs the temperature dependence of its spin coherence time (T2). Specifically, an optimal, intermediate diradical character minimizes coupling to local thermal phonons and maximizes T2 at elevated temperatures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of conjugated diradicaloids (e.g., based on triangulenes, zethrenes, or biradicaloid donor-acceptor systems) with systematically varied diradical character (y) quantified by symmetry-adapted cluster configuration interaction (SAC-CI) calculations and magnetic susceptibility measurements. Spin coherence times (T2) will be measured via pulsed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy across a temperature range (10-300 K). Advanced pulse sequences, such as Hahn echo and dynamical decoupling, will be employed to isolate intrinsic decoherence mechanisms. A closed feedback loop will correlate y with the measured T2(T) to extract design rules.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative experimental correlation between a fundamental chemical descriptor (diradical character) and the operational temperature of a molecular spin qubit. It will provide concrete molecular design principles for thermally resilient quantum coherence, moving beyond phenomenological optimization. The outcome will be a new class of chemically programmable quantum materials that function as coherent spin qubits at non-cryogenic temperatures.
proposals.novelty: While diradical character is known to influence photophysics and magnetism, its deliberate engineering for the specific purpose of high-temperature quantum coherence is a novel paradigm. This approach uniquely leverages ground-state electronic structure—a synthetic handle—to directly combat the primary limitation (thermal decoherence) in molecular quantum information science.

proposals.title: Tuning Spin Interactions and Exciton Energies in Radical-Based Semiconductors via Bridge and Radical Engineering
proposals.motivation: Organic radical semiconductors offer a unique platform for spin-optoelectronics due to their intrinsic doublet ground states and the formation of emissive spin-mixed quartet excitons. However, the fundamental relationships between molecular structure, spin-spin interactions, and exciton energy levels remain poorly understood, limiting the rational design of efficient devices. A systematic study is needed to establish design rules for controlling these properties, which are critical for applications in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) and quantum information science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the electronic coupling between radical centers, governed by the bridging moiety's conjugation length and topology, dictates the energy splitting between singlet and triplet charge-transfer (CT) states, which in turn controls the quartet exciton formation yield and its radiative decay rate. Furthermore, the choice of radical type (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl vs. perchlorotriphenylmethyl derivatives) will directly tune the doublet-quartet energy gap and photoluminescence quantum yield.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of donor-bridge-acceptor (D-B-A) molecules where the donor and acceptor are stable organic radicals (e.g., TTM and PTM derivatives). The bridging unit will be systematically varied from non-conjugated (e.g., bicyclo[1.1.1]pentane) to conjugated (e.g., phenyl, thienyl, fluorenyl) and cross-conjugated (e.g., meta-substituted phenyl) linkers. Steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy will characterize exciton energies and quartet lifetimes. Transient electron paramagnetic resonance (trEPR) and magneto-photoluminescence measurements will quantify spin-spin exchange interactions (J) and the quartet state population dynamics. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT calculations will model the electronic structures and predict exchange couplings.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive structure-property map linking bridge architecture and radical identity to spin interactions and exciton energies in radical semiconductors. It will provide concrete molecular design principles for achieving targeted quartet state energies and lifetimes, directly informing the development of high-efficiency, magnetically sensitive organic light-emitting devices and spin-based qubits.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has demonstrated quartet emission in specific radical pairs, a systematic exploration of the bridge as a primary tuning parameter for spin exchange and exciton energies is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond single-molecule optimization to create a predictive framework for a new class of organic quantum materials.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Heterogeneity in Luminescent Radicals: Correlating Photon Statistics with Spin-Dependent Triplet and Quartet Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Organic radical emitters, particularly those exhibiting efficient doublet and quartet state emission, show immense promise for next-generation optoelectronics. However, ensemble measurements mask intrinsic molecular heterogeneity in photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and spin-dependent decay rates, which critically impacts device performance and fundamental understanding. A systematic, single-molecule investigation is essential to deconvolute homogeneous broadening from static disorder and identify the molecular origins of performance-limiting processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the distribution of PLQY and emission lifetimes in radical emitters arises from static molecular variations in local environment and conformation, which differentially modulate the singlet-triplet energy gap (ΔEST) and the intersystem crossing rates between doublet, triplet, and quartet manifolds.
proposals.method: We will employ confocal fluorescence microscopy with Hanbury Brown–Twiss interferometry to perform single-molecule spectroscopy on isolated radical molecules (e.g., TTM-3PCz derivatives) dispersed in inert polymer matrices at cryogenic temperatures. Photon correlation statistics (g(2)(τ)) and time-tagged single-photon counting will be used to extract distributions of PLQY, emission lifetimes, and blinking dynamics. Magnetic field-dependent measurements (0-5 T) will be conducted to probe and correlate variations in spin-mixing efficiency and quartet state population kinetics at the single-particle level.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative map of heterogeneity in radical emitter photophysics, linking static disorder to spin-dependent processes. It will establish design rules for molecular engineering to suppress detrimental heterogeneity and identify 'champion' molecular conformations for hyperfluorescent OLEDs and quantum information applications based on spin states.
proposals.novelty: While single-molecule spectroscopy is established for conventional fluorophores, its application to correlated spin and emission dynamics in open-shell radical semiconductors is unprecedented. This approach uniquely targets the interplay between conformational disorder and the spin-addressable quartet states central to Friend's recent work.

proposals.title: One-Dimensional Spin-Photon Chains: Synthesis and Photophysics of Polymeric Diradical Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: Recent work on luminescent organic radicals, including from the Friend group, has demonstrated efficient light emission from doublet and quartet spin states, offering a pathway to circumvent spin-statistics limitations in OLEDs. However, these materials have been limited to small molecules or dimers. Extending this concept to one-dimensional polymeric chains of coupled diradicals could enable new cooperative quantum phenomena, enhanced spin-photon coupling, and materials with tunable magnetic and optoelectronic properties, a frontier unexplored before 2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing conjugated polymers with stable, covalently linked diradical repeat units will create a new class of one-dimensional spin-photon materials where intra-chain spin-spin interactions and exciton delocalization lead to emergent photophysical properties, such as amplified radiative rates from quartet manifolds and long-range spin coherence.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize donor-acceptor conjugated polymers where the acceptor unit is a stable, luminescent radical (e.g., a tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl derivative) dimerized via a conjugated bridge to form a repeating diradical motif. Polymerization will be achieved via Stille or Suzuki cross-coupling. We will characterize spin-spin coupling (J) using variable-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) magnetometry. Photophysics will be probed via temperature- and magnetic field-dependent photoluminescence (PL) and transient absorption spectroscopy to track quartet formation, intersystem crossing, and charge transfer dynamics along the chain.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first design principles for polymeric open-shell semiconductors, demonstrating control over one-dimensional spin correlations and their impact on light emission. It will provide a foundational platform for developing materials that unify quantum information (spin) and optoelectronics (photon) functionalities in a scalable, processable format.
proposals.novelty: While small-molecule luminescent radicals are known, the extension to a one-dimensional, covalently linked polymer of interacting diradicals, creating a macroscopic spin-photon chain, is a completely novel material concept with no reported examples prior to 2026.

proposals.title: Magnetic and Spin-Current Control of Exciton Spin-State Populations for Tunable Photoluminescence in Radical-Based Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: The ability to actively control the emission properties of organic semiconductors using non-electrical stimuli is a frontier challenge in optoelectronics. Recent work on luminescent organic radicals, particularly those exhibiting long-lived quartet states, has revealed a rich interplay between spin, charge, and optical transitions. This presents a unique opportunity to use external spin perturbations to manipulate exciton dynamics and emission, moving beyond static device engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applied magnetic fields and injected spin-polarized currents can selectively populate or depopulate specific excitonic spin states (singlet, triplet, doublet, quartet) in radical-based semiconductors, thereby providing direct, in-situ control over photoluminescence intensity and spectral characteristics.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize thin films of known luminescent radical semiconductors (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl-based derivatives) that support doublet and quartet states. Photoluminescence (PL) and electrically detected magnetic resonance (EDMR) measurements will be performed under variable external magnetic fields (0-1 T) and at cryogenic temperatures to isolate spin-dependent effects. For spin-current control, we will fabricate lateral device architectures where the radical semiconductor is interfaced with a ferromagnetic electrode (e.g., Co, Fe), enabling injection of spin-polarized charge carriers to perturb the local spin population and monitor consequent changes in PL intensity and spectrum via time-resolved and steady-state spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm of 'spin-optics' for organic semiconductors, demonstrating direct proof-of-concept for spin-based luminescence modulation. It will provide fundamental insights into the spin-dependent recombination pathways in radical excitons and quantify the coupling strength between external spin perturbations and optical output. This could inform the design of novel magneto-optical devices and spin-based sensors.
proposals.novelty: While magnetic field effects on electroluminescence are known, using them to *continuously tune* photoluminescence color and intensity in radical semiconductors is unexplored. Furthermore, the active use of a spin current, rather than just a magnetic field, to control emission constitutes a significant and non-obvious advance over existing spin-OLED research.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Confocal Spectroscopy of Diradical Spin Heterogeneity and Triplet-Quartet Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including from the Friend group, has established that organic diradicals exhibit unique photophysics, such as long-lived quartet states and intrinsic intermolecular charge separation, which are promising for quantum information and OLEDs. However, ensemble measurements average over molecular heterogeneity, obscuring the intrinsic relationship between molecular structure, local environment, and spin-state dynamics. Understanding this heterogeneity is critical for designing radical-based materials with predictable quantum properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single diradical molecules will exhibit a distribution of spin-state lifetimes and interconversion rates due to variations in local conformation and environment, and that direct observation will reveal discrete, molecule-specific pathways for triplet-to-quartet conversion and charge separation.
proposals.method: We will employ low-temperature confocal fluorescence microscopy with Hanbury Brown–Twiss interferometry to perform photon correlation spectroscopy on individual diradical molecules (e.g., TTM-3PCz derivatives) dispersed in an inert polymer matrix. A pulsed laser source (e.g., 405 nm) will be used for time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) and fluorescence lifetime imaging (FLIM). Advanced correlation algorithms, specifically second-order intensity correlation (g(2)(τ)) and time-tagged time-resolved (TTTR) data analysis, will quantify spin-state dynamics (singlet vs. quartet emission bursts) and identify anti-bunching signatures of single-photon emission from isolated spin states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, molecule-resolved map of spin heterogeneity in organic diradicals, quantifying the dispersion in quartet state yields and lifetimes. It will establish a foundational single-molecule methodology for radical semiconductors, directly linking molecular-scale disorder to macroscopic quantum efficiency and charge generation properties.
proposals.novelty: While single-molecule spectroscopy is mature for conventional fluorophores, its application to study spin-state heterogeneity and dynamics in open-shell diradical emitters is unprecedented. This moves beyond ensemble averaging to probe the intrinsic quantum photophysics of radical states at their most fundamental level.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Organic Diradical Emitters via DFT/MRCI: Engineering Spin-Optical Gaps for Efficient Triplet-to-Singlet Photon Harvesting
proposals.motivation: Organic diradicals offer a promising route to circumvent spin-statistical limits in OLEDs by enabling direct harvesting from triplet and quartet states. However, the rational design of stable diradicals with specific, tunable energy gaps between their spin manifolds (e.g., quartet-doublet or doublet-singlet) remains a major challenge. Pre-2026 work, including Friend's on luminescent radicals with long-lived quartet states, highlights the need for predictive tools to link molecular structure to spin-optical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced multireference quantum chemistry calculations can accurately predict the spin-state energetics and optical gaps of novel organic diradical structures, enabling the computational discovery of materials where the energy gap between the emissive doublet and a higher spin state (quartet or triplet) is minimized for efficient reverse intersystem crossing.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput computational screening workflow. Candidate diradical scaffolds will be generated by modifying known stable radical cores (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl derivatives) and novel bridged biradical architectures. For each candidate, geometry optimization and vibrational analysis will be performed using DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-TZVP). Subsequently, multireference calculations using the DFT/MRCI method will be implemented to accurately compute the energies of low-lying singlet, doublet, triplet, and quartet states, their spin-orbit couplings, and vertical excitation energies. The DMRG-NEVPT2 method will be used for validation on select promising candidates with strong multiconfigurational character.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated computational protocol for the *ab initio* design of organic diradicals with targeted spin-optical landscapes. It will yield specific molecular design rules and a shortlist of predicted high-performance emitters for synthesis, directly advancing the field of radical-based LEDs and quantum information materials. The project bridges high-level quantum chemistry with applied optoelectronic materials science.
proposals.novelty: While DFT/MRCI is established for closed-shell molecules, its systematic application to engineer the complex, multireference spin-state energetics of organic diradicals for optoelectronics is novel. This moves beyond the empirical tuning of known radicals to a predictive, first-principles design paradigm for spin-gap engineering.

proposals.title: Molecular Design of Optically Silent, Long-Lived Diradical Quartet States for Solid-State Spin Qubits
proposals.motivation: Organic molecular qubits offer a promising route to scalable quantum information processing, but their spin states are often vulnerable to decoherence via optical and magnetic interactions. Pre-2026 work on luminescent doublet radicals and intermolecular charge separation in Friend's group has demonstrated control over spin states and photophysics. This project aims to leverage this understanding to design the opposite: stable, optically dark spin qubits for memory, where long coherence is prioritized over optical readout.
proposals.hypothesis: By synthetically tailoring the exchange coupling and molecular symmetry in donor-acceptor diradicals, it is possible to isolate a long-lived, optically silent quartet ground state (S=1) that is protected from radiative decay and environmental magnetic noise, making it a viable candidate for a molecular spin memory qubit.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of donor-bridge-acceptor diradicals with strong intramolecular exchange, targeting a ground-state quartet. Using density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT, we will model electronic structure to predict exchange coupling (J) and ensure a large energy gap to optically active excited states. Solid-state pulsed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy, including Hahn echo sequences, will be used to experimentally characterize the spin coherence times (T2) of the quartet state, contrasting them with the photophysical properties measured via transient absorption spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for organic qubits, explicitly decoupling the memory function (requiring long T2) from the sensing/readout function (requiring optical activity). It will provide a library of characterized diradicals with quantified coherence times, advancing the materials toolkit for hybrid quantum architectures.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 organic qubit research focuses on optically addressable systems or doublet states, this proposal deliberately engineers a dark, high-spin quartet state for memory. This contrasts directly with the bright, sensing-oriented radical emitters recently developed in the field.

proposals.title: Bright, Low-Energy Photoluminescence from Triplet Ground State Diradicals via Zwitterionic Excited States
proposals.motivation: Organic radical emitters, particularly those with an open-shell singlet ground state, have emerged as promising materials for efficient OLEDs due to their spin-allowed doublet emission. However, the requirement for a specific open-shell singlet ground state limits the chemical space and stability of viable emitters. Exploring whether triplet ground state diradicals can also function as bright emitters would dramatically expand the design principles for radical-based optoelectronics, potentially unlocking new classes of stable, high-performance materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that certain organic diradicals with a triplet ground state can undergo a significant charge redistribution upon photoexcitation to form a low-energy, zwitterionic excited state, which can then radiatively decay with high efficiency, bypassing the typical spin-forbidden nature of triplet emission.
proposals.method: We will employ high-level quantum chemical calculations (e.g., CASSCF/NEVPT2, DMRG-SCF) to screen and design candidate triplet diradical molecules with a low-lying, charge-separated excited state. Promising candidates will be synthesized, and their photophysics will be characterized using ultrafast transient absorption and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy to directly probe the formation and decay of the zwitterionic state. Magnetic susceptibility measurements (SQUID) will confirm the triplet ground state, while electroluminescence studies will be conducted in simple OLED architectures to evaluate device performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for luminescent organic radicals, moving beyond the strict requirement for an open-shell singlet ground state. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between spin multiplicity, charge transfer, and radiative decay in open-shell systems, potentially leading to new emitters for displays and lighting with high efficiency and stability.
proposals.novelty: The proposal challenges the established dogma that a doublet ground state is essential for bright radical emission by targeting triplet ground state systems, a largely unexplored avenue for light emission. It uniquely combines the concepts of high-spin ground states and zwitterionic excited states, which have been studied separately but not linked for this purpose.

proposals.title: Direct Probing of Spin-Correlated Charge Dynamics in the Formation of Zwitterionic Excited States in Radical-Based Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that organic radical semiconductors exhibit long-lived, spin-polarized quartet states and intrinsic photoinduced charge separation, suggesting a complex interplay between spin, charge, and excited-state geometry. The formation of zwitterionic excited states, where significant intramolecular charge transfer occurs, is a key but poorly understood step in these systems, with its efficiency likely governed by spin-dependent interactions. Elucidating this link is critical for designing next-generation optoelectronic materials that exploit spin and charge degrees of freedom.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the formation yield and lifetime of a photogenerated zwitterionic state in donor-acceptor radical molecules are directly controlled by spin dynamics, specifically the interconversion between doublet, quartet, and singlet manifolds, which modulate electron-nuclear coupling and charge localization.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of donor-acceptor radical molecules with systematically varied electronic coupling. We will employ advanced magnetic spectroscopy techniques, primarily transient electron paramagnetic resonance (trEPR) and field-swept electrically detected magnetic resonance (EDMR), to directly monitor spin-state populations and coherence with sub-nanosecond resolution following photoexcitation. These measurements will be correlated with ultrafast optical spectroscopy (transient absorption and photoluminescence) to map spin dynamics onto the evolution of the excited-state absorption features characteristic of zwitterionic formation. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT calculations will model the potential energy surfaces of different spin states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental correlation between spin-state dynamics and zwitterionic excited-state formation in radical semiconductors. It will establish design rules for manipulating charge separation through spin engineering, directly informing the development of high-performance organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) based on hyperfluorescence and novel photodetectors or photovoltaic devices exploiting long-lived spin states.
proposals.novelty: While the photophysics of radical excited states is an emerging field, no study has yet applied trEPR/EDMR to directly resolve the spin dynamics *during* the critical charge-transfer event leading to a zwitterionic state, moving beyond static characterization of the resulting quartet or observing bulk device performance.

proposals.title: Mapping Zwitterionic Excited-State Dynamics and Spin-Exchange Couplings in Organic Radical Semiconductors via Ultrafast 2D Electronic Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that organic radical semiconductors exhibit intrinsic intermolecular photoinduced charge separation, forming long-lived, luminescent zwitterionic excited states with complex spin manifolds (e.g., quartet states). The energy transfer pathways, electronic couplings, and spin-dependent interactions that govern the formation and stability of these states remain poorly resolved spectroscopically. Understanding these dynamics is crucial for designing next-generation optoelectronic materials for light-emitting devices and quantum information science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ultrafast 2D electronic spectroscopy will directly reveal the correlated energy transfer pathways and coherent couplings between the initial photoexcited states and the long-lived zwitterionic/radical-pair states, and will spectroscopically resolve the influence of spin-exchange interactions (e.g., quartet formation) on these dynamics.
proposals.method: We will apply ultrafast two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy (2DES) with sub-20-fs time resolution to thin films of model radical semiconductors, such as (4-N-carbazolyl-2,6-dichlorophenyl)bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl radical. The experiment will use a passively phase-stable pump-probe geometry. We will analyze the 2D spectra using singular value decomposition (SVD) and global lifetime analysis to extract cross-peak dynamics, which map energy transfer. The evolution of diagonal and off-diagonal peak amplitudes as a function of waiting time will be modeled using a Redfield-theory-based approach to quantify electronic couplings and identify signatures of spin-state evolution (e.g., singlet-to-quartet conversion).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, time-resolved map of the electronic couplings and energy flow leading to the formation of zwitterionic excited states in radical semiconductors. It will establish a spectroscopic signature for spin-exchange interactions in the excited state, linking photophysical dynamics to the unique magnetic and luminescent properties of these materials. The findings will offer a new design principle for controlling charge separation and triplet harvesting in organic optoelectronics.
proposals.novelty: While 2DES is established for studying photosynthetic complexes and polymers, its application to resolve the spin-correlated, charge-separated dynamics in luminescent organic radicals is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond conventional transient absorption by directly probing the couplings between states that are spectrally overlapped and short-lived.

proposals.title: Mapping the Intermolecular Origins of Spin Mixing and Quartet Emission in Radical Dimers and Trimers
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated efficient photoluminescence and intermolecular charge separation in organic radical semiconductors, with long-lived quartet states emerging as key intermediates. However, in bulk films and polymers, the precise intermolecular interactions governing spin evolution from doublet to quartet and emissive singlet states remain obscured by structural and energetic disorder. A bottom-up approach using minimal, well-defined oligomeric units is essential to isolate and quantify these fundamental spin interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the photophysics of radical-based light emission and charge separation are governed by specific, short-range intermolecular geometries and electronic couplings that can be replicated and studied in synthetically tailored dimers and trimers, directly revealing the correlation between molecular arrangement, spin-state mixing, and luminescence efficiency.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of covalently linked dimers and trimers of established luminescent radical cores (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl derivatives), systematically varying the linker chemistry and conformation to control inter-radical distance and relative orientation. These discrete oligomers will be studied using ultrafast transient absorption and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy to track spin evolution (doublet→quartet→singlet). These results will be correlated with density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT calculations of exchange integrals and spin-orbit coupling matrix elements for the defined geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational structure-property relationship for spin interactions in organic radical assemblies, directly linking molecular architecture to quartet yield and radiative decay pathways. It will provide design rules for optimizing intermolecular interactions in solid-state radical semiconductors for both light-emitting and charge-transport applications, moving beyond empirical device optimization.
proposals.novelty: While bulk radical films have been studied, this proposal uniquely targets the *minimal quantum unit* of spin interaction—the dimer—using covalent synthesis to freeze-in and dissect geometries that are transient and averaged in disordered solids, offering unprecedented clarity on the quantum mechanical origins of their photophysics.

proposals.title: Triplet-Triplet Mediated Spin Interconversion in Acene-Radical Hybrids: A Pathway Beyond Charge Transfer States
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has established that photoinduced charge transfer (CT) states in organic radical-acene blends are crucial mediators for generating long-lived quartet states, enabling efficient spin interconversion and delayed fluorescence. However, the exclusive focus on CT-mediated pathways overlooks other potential spin-mixing channels. Understanding if and how direct triplet-triplet interactions between acene excitons and doublet radicals can mediate spin interconversion is essential for a complete physical picture and for designing next-generation radical-based optoelectronic materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that direct exchange and dipolar coupling between a localized acene triplet exciton and the radical's doublet ground state can efficiently mediate spin interconversion to form quartet and doublet excited states, providing a competitive pathway to the established CT-state-mediated mechanism.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of model systems pairing stable, luminescent radicals (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl) with acenes of varying triplet energy and spatial proximity, from covalently linked dyads to controlled blend films. Using transient electron paramagnetic resonance (trEPR) and magneto-photoluminescence, we will directly probe the spin multiplicity and dynamics of the initially formed states. Ultrafast spectroscopy will be used to temporally resolve the formation of triplet-pair or correlated triplet-doublet states prior to any charge separation, distinguishing this pathway from CT-mediated processes. Quantum chemical calculations (TD-DFT and CASSCF) will model the exchange interactions and spin energy levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new fundamental mechanism for spin interconversion in organic semiconductors, moving beyond the charge-transfer paradigm. It will provide design rules for exploiting direct triplet-radical interactions, potentially leading to materials with faster spin mixing or reduced reliance on specific donor-acceptor energetics for high-performance organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) and quantum information systems.
proposals.novelty: While CT-mediated spin mixing in radical systems is known, the explicit investigation of a direct, Coulomb-exchange-driven triplet-doublet interaction as a primary spin conversion pathway is novel and non-obvious, as conventional wisdom assumes CT state formation is a prerequisite for efficient interconversion.

proposals.title: Coherent Control and Rabi Oscillations of Photogenerated Quartet States in Organic Radical Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that organic radical semiconductors can support long-lived, high-spin quartet states following photoinduced charge separation. These states are promising for quantum information science due to their spin multiplicity and optical addressability. However, the quantum coherence properties and potential for coherent manipulation of these quartet states remain entirely unexplored, representing a critical gap in understanding their potential for spin-based quantum technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the quartet states in optimized donor-acceptor radical systems exhibit sufficiently long spin coherence times (T2) to allow their coherent quantum manipulation via resonant microwave pulses, and that their population can be driven into Rabi oscillations, demonstrating direct control over a four-level spin system.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize thin films of the recently reported high-performance radical emitters with long quartet lifetimes. Coherent manipulation will be probed using time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance (TR-EPR) and pulsed electrically detected magnetic resonance (pEDMR) spectroscopy at cryogenic temperatures. Specifically, we will apply a sequence of resonant microwave pulses (e.g., π/2 - τ - π echo sequences and direct Rabi oscillation protocols) to the photo-generated quartet states, measuring the resulting coherent spin dynamics. The experimental data will be modeled using a spin Hamiltonian incorporating zero-field splitting and hyperfine interactions to extract T2 times and Rabi frequencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the fundamental coherence limits of organic quartet states, providing the first metrics (T1, T2) essential for assessing their viability as quantum bits (qudits). It will demonstrate a new paradigm of optically initialized, electrically readable, and coherently controllable high-spin states in carbon-based materials, directly bridging photophysics, spintronics, and quantum control.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of quartet states is known, their coherent quantum control has never been attempted. This proposal moves beyond spectroscopic observation to active manipulation, targeting the demonstration of Rabi oscillations in an organic high-spin system, which is a non-obvious and foundational step toward functional molecular quantum units.

proposals.title: Generalizing Coulombic Control: A Systematic Investigation of Charge Transfer and Spin Dynamics in Diverse Luminescent Radical Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has established that Coulombic interactions critically govern photoinduced charge transfer and the formation of long-lived quartet states in luminescent radical semiconductors, exemplified by trityl derivatives. This discovery offers a powerful design rule for controlling spin and emission properties. However, it remains unknown whether this principle is specific to the trityl radical's electronic structure or represents a universal mechanism applicable to other radical classes, limiting the rational design of next-generation radical-based optoelectronic materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The Coulombic control of charge transfer and quartet state formation is a general phenomenon that can be applied to other stable luminescent radical families, such as perchlorotriphenylmethyl (PTM) radicals, Blatter's radicals, and phenalenyl derivatives, enabling predictable tuning of their photophysical and spin-dependent properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of donor-acceptor dyads where a carefully selected electron donor is covalently linked to distinct radical acceptor cores (PTM, Blatter's, phenalenyl). Using ultrafast transient absorption and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy, we will measure the charge transfer kinetics and quartet state yields. These experimental results will be correlated with quantum chemical calculations (TD-DFT and CASSCF) of the excited-state potential energy surfaces and Coulombic coupling matrix elements for each radical type, testing the predictive power of the established design rules.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the generality of Coulombic design principles, significantly expanding the toolkit for engineering radical-based materials. It will identify which radical classes are most amenable to this control strategy, paving the way for new high-performance materials for organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), spin-based information processing, and photonic quantum technologies.
proposals.novelty: While Coulombic control has been demonstrated for one radical system, its systematic validation across fundamentally different radical architectures with varying spin densities and frontier orbital distributions is a critical and unexplored step toward a unified design theory for organic radical semiconductors.

proposals.title: Quantum Photonic Interfaces Using High-Yield Organic Quartet Emitters
proposals.motivation: The development of practical quantum technologies requires efficient, scalable sources of photonic qubits and quantum memories. Organic radical semiconductors with high-yield, long-lived quartet states, as demonstrated in recent work, offer a unique combination of spin-addressable quantum states and efficient optical emission, but they have not been integrated into functional quantum device architectures. Bridging this gap could unlock new material platforms for quantum information science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spin-polarized, photostable luminescence from high-yield quartet states in organic radical emitters can be coupled to photonic nanostructures to create a new class of on-demand, spin-photon interfaces for quantum networks.
proposals.method: We will fabricate thin films of high-yield quartet emitters (e.g., TTM-3PCz derivatives) via thermal evaporation onto pre-patterned silicon nitride photonic crystal cavities and ring resonators. We will employ time-resolved photoluminescence and Hanle-effect measurements under magnetic field to characterize spin-dependent emission into cavity modes. The coupling efficiency and spin initialization fidelity will be quantified using pulsed optical excitation and time-correlated single-photon counting with polarization analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-principle for using organic quartet states as spin-photon interfaces, directly contributing to the field of molecular quantum photonics. It will provide the first metrics on spin-photon coupling efficiency and coherence for these materials, guiding future design of organic quantum devices.
proposals.novelty: While quartet states have been studied for OLEDs, their deliberate integration into photonic quantum circuits to exploit their spin properties is unexplored. This moves beyond using these materials solely for lighting, repurposing their intrinsic quantum mechanical properties for information processing.

proposals.title: Spin-State Topology in Multi-Orbital Organic Diradicals: Extending the Quartet Model Beyond Two-Electron Systems
proposals.motivation: The recent discovery of long-lived, luminescent quartet states in radical-based organic semiconductors, pioneered by the Friend group, has established a new paradigm for spin manipulation in optoelectronics. However, the foundational understanding of these states is built upon a simplified two-orbital, two-electron model, which may not capture the complex spin physics of real molecular systems with multiple active orbitals and electron correlation. This gap limits the rational design of next-generation materials with tailored spin properties for quantum information and OLED applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in diradicals with multi-orbital electronic structures, the conventional singlet-triplet-quartet manifold evolves into a more complex spin-state topology, where state mixing, higher-order spin multiplicities (e.g., quintets, sextets), and orbital symmetry dictate photophysical properties and charge transfer dynamics.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of stable, conjugated diradicals with systematically varied orbital topologies (e.g., linear vs. cyclic, orthogonal vs. non-orthogonal orbitals). Advanced magneto-optic spectroscopy (time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance and magnetic field-effect photoluminescence) will be employed to map spin-state populations and coherence. These experimental results will be integrated with high-level multi-configurational quantum chemistry calculations (CASSCF/NEVPT2) to construct accurate spin-Hamiltonians and identify the key orbital and exchange parameters governing the emergent spin-state landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental and theoretical framework for understanding spin physics in multi-orbital organic diradicals, moving beyond the foundational two-electron model. It will establish design rules for accessing and stabilizing high-spin states, directly informing the development of novel materials for spin-based OLEDs, molecular qubits, and spin-polarized charge transport layers.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of quartet states in two-electron diradicals is established, the systematic exploration of how spin-state categories break down or transform in more complex, multi-orbital systems is entirely unexplored. This project uniquely bridges molecular quantum chemistry with device-relevant photophysics to probe this new frontier.

proposals.title: Solvent-Stabilized Zwitterionic States for High-Temperature Coherent Spin Control in Molecular Radicals
proposals.motivation: Achieving coherent quantum control of molecular spins at elevated temperatures is a critical challenge for quantum sensing and information technologies. Pre-2026 work, including Friend's studies on long-lived quartet states in luminescent radicals, shows that charge-separated states can have favorable spin properties, but they are often thermally unstable. Simultaneously, the role of the solvent environment in stabilizing zwitterionic states and modulating spin-lattice interactions remains an underexplored avenue for engineering spin coherence.
proposals.hypothesis: That polar solvent environments can be rationally designed to stabilize specific zwitterionic electronic configurations in organic radical semiconductors, thereby tailoring the spin Hamiltonian and creating a protective solvation shell that suppresses local magnetic noise, enabling coherent microwave manipulation of spin states at temperatures above 77 K.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of donor-acceptor molecular radicals known to support intramolecular charge-transfer states. Using time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance (TR-EPR) and transient optical spectroscopy, we will map the correlation between solvent polarity/dielectric constant and the population yield/lifetime of the zwitterionic quartet state. Coherent spin control will be tested via pulsed EPR (DEER, Hahn echo) on solvent-frozen matrices to measure phase memory times (Tm) as a function of temperature and solvent engineering. The spin Hamiltonian will be modeled using density functional theory (DFT) calculations incorporating polarizable continuum models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for 'solvent bath engineering' as a direct materials design strategy for molecular quantum technologies. It will provide a concrete pathway to achieve operational temperatures for molecular spin qubits and sensors significantly closer to practical, non-cryogenic conditions, directly linking the fields of organic spintronics, photophysics, and quantum control.
proposals.novelty: While solvent effects on excited states are well-known, their deliberate use as a tunable 'spin bath' to enhance coherence by stabilizing a specific charge-separated spin manifold (e.g., a quartet) is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of photophysical and quantum spin control concepts.

proposals.title: Stable Open-Shell Ground States in Organic Radical Semiconductors via Steric and Electronic Encapsulation
proposals.motivation: Organic open-shell molecules with pure spin ground states (e.g., singlets or triplets) are promising for quantum information and spintronics, but their inherent reactivity and sensitivity to environment often preclude their isolation and use as processable materials. Pre-2026 work on luminescent radicals and intermolecular charge separation has highlighted the potential of long-lived quartet states, yet stable, well-defined open-shell ground states in solid-state materials remain a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a three-dimensional, rigid molecular scaffold, designed with specific steric bulk and electronic properties, can kinetically protect a predesigned open-shell core, enforce a specific spin multiplicity as the unambiguous ground state, and yield an air-stable, processable semiconductor.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize molecules featuring a stable radical or diradical core (e.g., derived from tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl or triangulene derivatives) covalently locked within a sterically demanding, shape-persistent encapsulating structure (e.g., based on triptycene, spirobifluorene, or dendritic aryl groups). Electronic structure will be tuned via substituents to control exchange coupling and ensure a singlet or triplet ground state. Stability and photophysics will be characterized by SQUID magnetometry, EPR, transient absorption spectroscopy, and device fabrication for charge transport studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general design principle for creating stable, processable organic materials with well-defined spin ground states. It will directly contribute to the field of organic spintronics by providing new molecular candidates for spin transport and potentially enable the study of coherent spin phenomena in solid-state radical-based devices.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond simple diradical design or host-guest systems, this approach integrates kinetic stability and ground-state spin control into a single, covalently bound molecular architecture, aiming for materials that are stable under ambient conditions—a key step towards practical applications.

proposals.title: Mapping Topological Connectivity to Long-Range Spin Correlations in π-Conjugated Polyradical Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Organic radical semiconductors show immense promise for quantum information and spintronics due to their long-lived, photoaccessible quartet states and intermolecular charge separation. However, moving from isolated diradicals or small clusters to extended one- and two-dimensional architectures presents a fundamental challenge: we lack design rules for how local connectivity and conjugation topology dictate the emergence of cooperative quantum states like spin frustration or topological magnon bands, which are critical for device functionality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the global spin Hamiltonian and emergent quantum properties of a polyradical network are not simple sums of pairwise interactions but are instead dictated by the topological connectivity of the radical centers, which can be engineered through specific linker motifs and heteroatom substitutions to produce designer spin correlations and band structures.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput DFT and post-Hartree-Fock calculations (using methods like CASSCF/NEVPT2) to screen a library of model 1D chains and 2D nets, systematically varying linker topology (e.g., linear, kinked, macrocyclic), conjugation pattern (cross-conjugated vs. linearly conjugated), and heteroatom doping. This computational mapping will be validated by synthesizing key predicted structures using controlled, stepwise coupling of stable radical building blocks (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl radical derivatives), with their electronic structures probed via transient electron paramagnetic resonance and magneto-optical spectroscopy to measure spin-spin interactions and coherence times.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first concrete set of topological design principles for engineering long-range spin correlations in organic materials, moving beyond heuristic diradical character descriptions. It will directly enable the rational design of organic quantum materials with tailored spin Hamiltonians for applications in topological magnonics and coherent spin transport.
proposals.novelty: While previous work correlates local structure with diradical or quartet state yield, this project uniquely focuses on the *topological* propagation of interactions in extended systems, treating the network connectivity as a primary variable to sculpt the global quantum mechanical wavefunction, a perspective largely unexplored in organic radical materials pre-2026.

proposals.title: Systematic Mapping of Diradical Character, Spin State Energetics, and Optical Properties in Organic Radical Semiconductors
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that organic radical semiconductors exhibit unique photophysics, including long-lived quartet states and efficient intermolecular charge separation, which are promising for OLEDs and quantum technologies. However, the fundamental relationships between specific molecular structural motifs, the resulting diradical character categories (e.g., open-shell singlet vs. triplet ground states), and key optical properties remain poorly understood and largely unmapped. A systematic investigation is needed to move beyond serendipitous discovery and enable the rational design of radical-based materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted modifications to molecular core structure, donor/acceptor strength, and steric hindrance will predictably tune the energetic ordering of singlet, triplet, and quartet spin states, which in turn governs photoluminescence quantum yield, emission wavelength, and charge-transfer dynamics in radical emitters.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of radical molecules (e.g., based on tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl (TTM) and perchlorotriphenylmethyl (PTM) cores) with systematic variations in conjugation length, substituent electronic character, and dimerization geometry. Each derivative will be characterized using variable-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) magnetometry to quantify spin state populations and exchange couplings. These results will be correlated with ultrafast transient absorption and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy to map spin-dependent relaxation pathways. Multivariate analysis and machine learning (specifically, kernel ridge regression models using molecular descriptors from DFT calculations) will be used to establish quantitative structure-property relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive dataset directly linking chemical structure to spin-state manifolds and photophysical outcomes in organic diradicals. It will provide design rules for stabilizing high-spin quartet states for quantum applications or for optimizing luminescent doublet states for efficient hyperfluorescent OLEDs. The resulting predictive framework will accelerate the discovery of next-generation radical-based semiconductors.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have identified individual high-performing radicals, this proposal systematically decouples and quantifies the influence of distinct structural parameters on the complex interplay between diradical categories (open-shell singlet vs. triplet) and their photophysics, moving from observation to prediction.

proposals.title: Controlling Singlet Fission Pathways: Mapping Conical Intersections in a Tunable Series of Diradical Chromophores
proposals.motivation: Singlet fission (SF) can boost photovoltaic efficiencies, but its quantum yield is limited by competing non-radiative pathways. The initial photoexcitation can evolve through distinct diradical, zwitterionic, or multiexciton states, but the key conical intersections (CIs) that govern the branching between these channels remain poorly mapped. Understanding these non-adiabatic funnels is critical for rationally designing efficient SF materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic tuning of a chromophore's diradical character will directly control the energetic landscape and accessibility of specific conical intersections, thereby dictating the branching ratio between desired correlated triplet-pair formation and lossy charge-transfer or diradical pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of SF candidate molecules (e.g., based on pentacene derivatives or biradicaloids) with controlled diradical character via chemical substitution. High-level multireference calculations (CASSCF/NEVPT2 with state-averaged orbitals) will map the multidimensional potential energy surfaces of the lowest excited singlet states and locate CIs. These predictions will guide ultrafast spectroscopic experiments, specifically femtosecond stimulated Raman spectroscopy (FSRS) to track vibrational coherences and 2D electronic spectroscopy (2DES) to resolve wavepacket dynamics and branching in real time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, molecule-by-molecule correlation between computed CI topologies and observed ultrafast branching dynamics in SF. It will establish clear design rules for steering photogenerated excitons towards the triplet-pair state, moving beyond static electronic structure to dynamic control. This advances the fundamental understanding of multiexciton generation in organic semiconductors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, adversarial feedback between theory and experiment: using calculated CIs to design spectroscopic observables, then using experimental dynamics to validate and refine the theoretical models of the non-adiabatic landscape, a synergy rarely achieved in complex molecular photophysics.

proposals.title: Ultrafast Non-Adiabatic Pathways in Open-Shell Organic Semiconductors: Mapping Diradical, Zwitterionic, and Biexciton State Interconversion
proposals.motivation: The recent discovery of long-lived, luminescent quartet states in radical-based organic semiconductors opens new avenues for optoelectronics. However, the fundamental photophysics governing the formation and interconversion of these multi-excitonic states from photoexcited diradical or zwitterionic precursors remains poorly understood. A detailed map of the non-adiabatic transitions between these correlated electronic states is critical for rationally designing materials with controlled spin dynamics for applications in OLEDs, quantum information, and photochemical conversion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the branching ratio between long-lived emissive states (e.g., doublet or quartet) and non-radiative decay channels in open-shell systems is governed by specific, ultrafast conical intersections connecting diradical, charge-separated zwitterionic, and biexciton potential energy surfaces.
proposals.method: We will employ high-level ab initio quantum chemistry (CASSCF/NEVPT2) to map the potential energy surfaces of model diradical systems. Non-adiabatic coupling vectors and minimum energy conical intersection (MECI) geometries will be calculated between key states. These static electronic structure results will inform surface-hopping molecular dynamics simulations (e.g., SHARC) to model the ultrafast (<1 ps) inter-state dynamics following photoexcitation. The computational predictions will be validated using ultrafast broadband transient absorption spectroscopy, with particular attention to signatures of charge transfer and spin multiplicity changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dynamic picture of multi-excitonic state interconversion in open-shell organic semiconductors, directly linking molecular structure to spin-state evolution. It will establish design rules for manipulating the yield of long-lived luminescent states versus charge-separated or non-radiative pathways, thereby advancing the development of radical-based light-emitting devices and spin-photonic materials.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified stable radical states, the explicit investigation of the *non-adiabatic transitions* between distinct diradical categories (covalent vs. ionic) and their multi-exciton products represents a novel and necessary step beyond static electronic structure characterization.

proposals.title: Autonomous Quantum State Control in Diradical OLEDs via Real-Time Machine Learning Feedback
proposals.motivation: Organic luminescent radicals, particularly those with long-lived quartet states, offer a unique platform for solid-state quantum technologies like magnetometry and spin-photon interfaces. However, their performance in devices is limited by the uncontrolled interconversion between singlet, doublet, and triplet manifolds under operational conditions. A method to actively stabilize a desired quantum state within a working device would unlock new functionality and efficiency, a critical pre-2026 challenge in organic spintronics and quantum sensing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system integrating real-time optical spectroscopy with a pre-trained machine learning classifier can dynamically identify and stabilize specific diradical excited states (e.g., the emissive doublet or long-lived quartet) within an operational organic light-emitting diode (OLED) by adjusting electrical and optical device parameters.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a microcavity OLED using a high-performance luminescent diradical emitter, such as a tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl-based derivative, with integrated photoluminescence and electroluminescence spectroscopic readout. A convolutional neural network (CNN), pre-trained on a dataset of simulated emission spectra linked to computed wavefunction characteristics (e.g., spin density, charge transfer character), will classify the dominant excited state from the real-time spectral output. This classification will feed into a PID controller that adjusts the applied bias or pulse sequence to maximize the spectral signature of the target state, creating a feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first autonomous device that optimizes its own quantum state population for a defined task, moving beyond static material design. It will provide a new experimental platform to study and control spin dynamics in operational radical-based devices, directly impacting the development of organic quantum sensors and spin-LEDs.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been used for materials discovery and spectroscopy analysis, its integration as a real-time control element for stabilizing specific molecular quantum states within a functioning optoelectronic device is a novel paradigm. This approach fuses quantum chemistry, device engineering, and control theory in a uniquely integrated manner.

proposals.title: Microscopic Solvent Control of Diradical Character and Spin States via Environmentally-Embedded DMRG
proposals.motivation: Organic diradicals and biradicaloids are promising for quantum technologies and spintronics, but their electronic structure is exquisitely sensitive to environment, as shown by Friend's work on photoinduced charge separation in radical semiconductors. Current computational methods, like gas-phase DMRG or continuum solvation models, fail to capture the atomistic, quantum-mechanical coupling between a strongly correlated diradical and specific solvent molecules, limiting our understanding of solvent-induced state switching.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly embedding key solvent molecules or a polarizable environment into the DMRG active space will reveal that local, directional solute-solvent interactions are the primary driver for stabilizing zwitterionic configurations, dramatically altering diradical character, spin-spin correlations, and the energetic ordering of singlet, triplet, and open-shell quartet states.
proposals.method: We will develop an environmentally-embedded DMRG (EE-DMRG) framework. The active system will comprise the diradical core plus a minimal set of explicit solvent molecules or a polarizable quantum mechanical (QM) region described by an accurate basis set. The full Hamiltonian will include the diradical's electronic interactions and their explicit coupling to the embedded environment's electrons. We will use a state-averaged DMRG algorithm to compute ground and excited states, quantifying changes in diradical character (y0), spin-spin correlation functions, and orbital entanglement entropy as a function of the embedded environment's geometry and polarity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantum-mechanically rigorous maps connecting specific solvent configurations to diradical electronic structure and spin-state energetics. It will deliver predictive design rules for environment-stable radical pairs in organic semiconductors and establish a new computational paradigm for studying correlated open-shell systems in complex, atomistic environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of high-level, active-space DMRG for strong correlation with an atomistically explicit, quantum-mechanically treated environment, moving decisively beyond the implicit dielectric continuum or mechanical embedding approaches standard in the field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Mapping Diradical versus Zwitterionic Character in Solid-State Defects via Pulsed EPR for Quantum Coherence Engineering
proposals.motivation: Paramagnetic defects in semiconductors and 2D materials are promising spin qubit candidates, but their quantum coherence is limited by spin-environment interactions. Pre-2026, the electronic structure of such defects—whether they are open-shell diradicals (correlated spins) or closed-shell zwitterions (ionic)—is predicted to fundamentally dictate their hyperfine coupling and spin dephasing, yet this link remains experimentally unverified. This gap hinders the rational design of materials with long-lived spin states for quantum technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pulsed EPR spectroscopy can experimentally distinguish diradical from zwitterionic defect character, and that diradical-dominated states, with more delocalized and correlated spin density, will exhibit longer spin coherence times (T2) due to reduced hyperfine interactions with local nuclear spins.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and isolate model defect systems in wide-bandgap organic semiconductors (e.g., in tailored acene derivatives) and 2D materials (e.g., hBN). Advanced pulsed EPR techniques, specifically Hyperfine Sublevel Correlation (HYSCORE) spectroscopy, will be used to map electron-nuclear hyperfine and quadrupole tensors, quantifying local spin density and polarity. Electron Spin Echo Envelope Modulation (ESEEM) and Hahn echo decay measurements will directly probe the hyperfine coupling strength and extract spin coherence times (T2). These experimental parameters will be correlated with ab initio calculations (DFT/MRCI) of the defect's diradical/zwitterionic character using valence-bond theory descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental framework for classifying solid-state defect electronic structure using spin spectroscopy, moving beyond theoretical prediction. It will directly link a defect's fundamental electronic character to its performance as a quantum bit, providing clear materials design rules. This will accelerate the discovery of organic and 2D material hosts for high-coherence spin qubits.
proposals.novelty: While pulsed EPR is used to study spin dynamics, its systematic application to classify defect electronic structure via the diradical/zwitterionic paradigm is novel. The fusion of valence-bond theory concepts with quantum coherence metrics creates a new predictive design loop for quantum materials.

proposals.title: Correlated Spin States and Charge Transport in Conjugated Polyradical Chains and Networks
proposals.motivation: Recent work on doublet and quartet states in luminescent organic radicals has revealed unique photophysics, such as long-lived triplet-pair (quartet) states and intrinsic photoinduced charge separation. However, these studies are limited to molecules with one or two radical centers. Extending this to polyradical systems is crucial to understand how multiple, interacting radical sites influence spin correlations, energy levels, and charge transport, potentially unlocking new quantum materials and spintronic functionalities.
