proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in conjugated polyradical chains and networks, the interplay between through-bond exchange coupling and through-space dipolar interactions will lead to emergent, correlated spin manifolds beyond simple doublet/quartet classifications, which will fundamentally alter exciton dynamics and charge transport pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of well-defined conjugated oligomers and polymers with stable, pendant radical centers (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl radicals) at controlled intervals. We will employ ultrafast transient absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy, complemented by continuous-wave and pulsed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy, to map the spin-state evolution and dynamics. Charge transport properties will be probed using field-effect transistor and time-of-flight measurements on thin-film devices, correlating mobility with radical density and coupling geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property relationships for photoexcited states and charge transport in conjugated polyradicals. It will provide a foundational understanding of how to engineer spin correlations in organic materials, directly informing the design of next-generation organic spintronic devices and materials for quantum information science.
proposals.novelty: While the photophysics of mono- and di-radicals are being explored, the systematic investigation of correlated spin states and their functional consequences in extended polyradical architectures is entirely unexplored, moving beyond molecular entities to tunable solid-state quantum systems.

proposals.title: Quantifying and Engineering the Diradical-to-Polyradical Continuum in Organic Networks for Spin-Enhanced Optoelectronics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the Friend group has pioneered the understanding of spin-mixed states (e.g., quartets) in organic radical semiconductors, revealing their potential for long-lived charge separation and efficient light emission. However, a fundamental gap exists between the study of discrete, localized diradicaloid 'units' and the emergent properties of extended systems with correlated spins. A systematic framework to connect these regimes is needed to rationally design materials with tailored magnetic, conductive, and excitonic properties from the bottom up.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diradical character of a molecular building block, quantified as a continuous variable, acts as a 'seed' spin density whose intermolecular coupling in extended networks can be predictively tuned to generate a continuum of electronic structures, from closed-shell semiconductors to open-shell polyradicals with high-spin ground states.
proposals.method: We will first quantify the diradical character (y) of candidate closed-shell chromophores (e.g., acenes, zethrenes) using high-level multireference calculations (CASSCF/NEVPT2). These characterized units will be synthetically linked into 1D chains and 2D covalent organic frameworks (COFs) via conjugated bridges of varying topology. The emergent electronic structure will be mapped using a combination of ultrafast spectroscopy (to track spin dynamics and exciton fission), variable-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) for spin multiplicity, and electrically detected magnetic resonance (EDMR) in device structures to probe spin-charge interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for organic spin materials, moving beyond binary classification to a continuous 'diradical index' engineering approach. It will directly link molecular quantum chemistry to the macroscopic electronic and magnetic properties of thin films, providing a roadmap for creating materials with on-demand combinations of conductivity, luminescence, and magnetic order.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating diradicalicity not as a static molecular property but as a tunable, interacting degree of freedom in the solid state, creating a unified experimental and theoretical continuum between the well-studied limits of singlet excitonics and high-spin polyradical magnetism.

proposals.title: Inverse Quantum Design of Diradical Emitters via Targeted Control of Triplet-Quartet State Manifolds
proposals.motivation: The development of organic diradicals and radical-based semiconductors for optoelectronics is hindered by the empirical challenge of tuning their complex, multi-configurational excited states. Pre-2026 work, including from the Friend group, has shown that long-lived quartet states and intermolecular charge transfer in radicals offer new pathways for light emission and photoconversion. A systematic, predictive method to design diradicals with a specific optical gap is critically needed to unlock their potential for tailored OLEDs and photophysical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing the molecular structure of a diradical as a set of quantum control parameters, an inverse design algorithm can efficiently identify chemical motifs that produce a target excited-state energy gap, specifically by engineering the singlet-triplet-quartet state mixture.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline combining density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) calculations for accurate diradical ground states with time-dependent DFT for excited states. A generative neural network, trained on a dataset of known diradicals, will propose candidate structures. A Bayesian optimization loop will then iteratively adjust 'control' parameters—bridge topology, donor/acceptor strength, and steric bulk—to minimize the difference between the calculated lowest optical transition (e.g., quartet-to-doublet) and the target gap, directly analogous to quantum optimal control theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational tool for the targeted design of open-shell organic materials, moving beyond heuristic rules. It will generate specific, testable molecular designs for diradicals with predetermined optical properties, directly informing synthesis efforts for next-generation radical-based LEDs and spin-photonic devices.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying the formal inverse problem framework of quantum coherent control to molecular electronic structure design, treating chemical subunits as analogous to control laser pulses to shape many-body diradical states.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Diradical Emitters: Targeting Specific Optical Gaps via Controlled Singlet-Triplet-Quartet State Mixing
proposals.motivation: The rational design of organic radical emitters for optoelectronics, particularly OLEDs, is currently limited by a forward-design paradigm where molecular structure is tweaked to observe resulting excited-state energies. This is inefficient for targeting specific optical gaps required for applications like deep blue emission or near-infrared sensors. An inverse-design approach, starting from a desired excited-state energy and computationally deriving candidate structures, would dramatically accelerate the discovery of high-performance radical materials.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically controlling the exchange interaction (J) and diradical character (y) in donor-acceptor diradical architectures, we can inversely engineer molecular structures to achieve a target optical gap, defined by the energy of the lowest allowed transition from a mixed singlet-triplet-quartet manifold.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline combining density functional theory (DFT) and multireference methods (e.g., CASSCF/NEVPT2) to map the relationship between molecular descriptors (topology, linker conjugation, donor/acceptor strength) and the resulting excited-state energies and compositions. A generative machine learning model, built on a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture, will be trained on this dataset. The model will be conditioned on a target optical gap and desired spin-state admixture (e.g., high quartet yield for long-lived luminescence) to propose novel diradical structures, which will then be validated with high-level electronic structure calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-principles, inverse-design framework for organic radical semiconductors, moving the field beyond serendipitous discovery. It will yield a set of design rules and predicted candidate molecules with tailored optical gaps and spin properties, directly informing the synthesis of next-generation emitters for hyperfluorescent OLEDs and spin-optoelectronic devices.
proposals.novelty: While inverse design is established for closed-shell molecules, applying it to the complex, open-shell spin landscape of diradicals—where optical gaps emerge from subtle mixtures of singlet, triplet, and quartet states—is a fundamentally new and unexplored challenge.

proposals.title: A Predictive Descriptor Framework for the Rational Design of Functional Triplet Diradicals
proposals.motivation: Triplet diradicals are promising for organic spintronics and catalysis, but their development is hindered by a lack of predictive design principles. Current work, including our own on quartet states and intermolecular charge separation, provides a descriptive classification but not a quantitative link between molecular structure and functional properties like spin-state stability and magnetic coupling. A predictive map is needed to move from serendipitous discovery to targeted synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantitative map linking key quantum chemical descriptors—such as diradical character (y), singlet-triplet energy gap (ΔEST), and spatial distribution of unpaired electrons—to structural motifs will enable the rational design of triplet diradicals with tailored magnetic and electronic properties.
proposals.method: We will first curate a dataset of known triplet diradicals, calculating descriptors using density functional theory (DFT) and post-Hartree-Fock methods (e.g., CASSCF/NEVPT2). Machine learning models (e.g., kernel ridge regression, graph neural networks) will then be trained to predict ΔEST and coupling constants from molecular graphs and descriptors. The validated model will be used to screen a large virtual chemical space, proposing new candidates with targeted properties, which will be validated via high-level DMRG or multireference calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available predictive framework that directly connects diradical structure to function, moving the field beyond taxonomy. It will generate specific, computationally validated design rules and a set of novel candidate molecules with predicted enhanced properties for spin-based applications, directly informing future synthetic efforts.
proposals.novelty: While classification schemes exist, this project uniquely integrates quantum descriptors with machine learning to create a predictive, rather than descriptive, tool for diradical design. It directly leverages our group's expertise in radical states and spin dynamics to solve the inverse design problem for these materials.

proposals.title: Optical Generation and Detection of Photogenerated Quintet States in Blended Radical:Acceptor Systems
proposals.motivation: The recent discovery of long-lived, luminescent doublet and quartet states in radical-based semiconductors opens a new frontier in organic spintronics and quantum information science. However, access to high-spin manifolds (S ≥ 2) remains unexplored, representing a fundamental limit for spin-based logic and memory. Achieving and optically reading out quintet states would dramatically expand the available spin multiplicity for quantum technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by photoexciting a blend of a stable luminescent radical (as a doublet ground state donor) with a high-electron-affinity, spin-1 acceptor molecule, we can generate a correlated triplet-triplet pair that can fuse into a quintet biexciton, which will exhibit distinct, detectable photoluminescence.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and blend a high-performance TADF radical emitter (e.g., a carbene-metal-amide derivative) with a carefully chosen triplet-generating acceptor, such as a palladium porphyrin. Using ultrafast transient absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy with time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance (TR-EPR), we will track spin evolution from the initial charge-separated state to the triplet pair and finally to the quintet. Magnetic field effects on photoluminescence will provide a direct optical signature of quintet formation and decay.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental protocol for generating and optically detecting quintet states in an organic solid-state system. It will provide fundamental insights into spin-statistics and exchange interactions in high-multiplicity manifolds, paving the way for quintet-based spin qubits and high-density molecular spin memory.
proposals.novelty: While quartet states have been recently observed in radicals, the intentional design of a donor-acceptor system to access and optically probe the quintet spin surface (S=2) is a completely unexplored and non-trivial extension of existing radical photophysics.

proposals.title: Systematic Exploration of Stable Non-Luminescent Radicals as Spin-Selective Charge Transfer Mediators in TADF-Radical Hybrid Systems
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that appending the stable radical TEMPO to TADF chromophores enables spin-selective intermolecular charge transfer, leading to long-lived quartet states and efficient light emission. This presents a novel strategy for controlling spin and charge in organic semiconductors. However, the generality of this approach beyond the TEMPO radical is unknown, limiting the design space and fundamental understanding of the radical-TADF interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: That the unique photophysical effects observed in TEMPO-TADF systems—specifically, spin-selective charge transfer and quartet state formation—are a general phenomenon achievable with other stable, non-luminescent radicals, and that the electronic structure and redox potential of the radical critically determine the efficiency of this process.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of donor-acceptor TADF chromophores (e.g., based on carbazole-benzonitrile or acridine-triazine cores) and covalently attach them to a library of stable radicals beyond TEMPO. This library will include nitroxides (e.g., PROXYL, 2,2,5,5-tetramethyl-1-pyrrolidinyloxy), verdazyl radicals, and phenoxyl radicals, chosen for their varied redox potentials and steric profiles. The photophysics of these hybrids will be characterized using transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-ns timescales) to track charge separation and quartet formation, photoluminescence quantum yield measurements, and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy to directly probe spin states and dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-property relationship between radical identity and the efficiency of spin-selective processes in TADF-radical hybrids. It will expand the toolkit for designing organic quantum materials with controlled multi-spin states and identify new radical candidates that may outperform TEMPO for specific applications in opto-spintronics or as sensitizers in hyperfluorescent OLEDs.
proposals.novelty: While the TEMPO-TADF interaction is a recent discovery, this proposal systematically explores the chemical space of stable radicals to test the universality of the mechanism, moving from a single exemplary system to a foundational design principle for a new class of spin-active organic semiconductors.

proposals.title: Systematic Donor Engineering in Luminescent Radical Emitters to Control Triplet-Quartet Mixing and Suppress Non-Radiative Loss
proposals.motivation: Organic luminescent radicals, particularly those exhibiting long-lived quartet states, offer a unique pathway to circumvent spin-statistical limits in electroluminescence. However, non-radiative decay pathways, often mediated by high-energy vibrational modes from specific donor units, remain a major bottleneck for device efficiency and operational stability. A systematic study correlating donor structure with photophysical properties in these open-shell systems is critically needed but lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategic modification of the donor unit in donor-acceptor luminescent radical scaffolds will provide fine control over the energy alignment and coupling between local triplet (3LE) and doublet-quartet (2CT/4CT) states, thereby directly influencing the quartet yield and suppressing dominant non-radiative decay channels.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a homologous series of radical emitters based on a stable tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl (TTM) or similar acceptor core, systematically varying the donor unit from carbazole to acridan, phenoxazine, and phenothiazine derivatives. Photophysical characterization will include temperature-dependent photoluminescence, transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-ns), and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) to map excited state dynamics. Quantum chemical calculations (TD-DFT) will be employed to model the electronic structure, spin density distributions, and vibronic coupling for each derivative.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first clear structure-property relationships for donor-acceptor radicals, providing a molecular design rulebook for maximizing photoluminescence quantum yield via quartet state management. It will deliver new, optimized radical emitters with reduced non-radiative losses, directly informing the development of next-generation, high-efficiency radical-based OLEDs and spin-optoelectronic devices.
proposals.novelty: While donor engineering is common in closed-shell TADF systems, its application to control the complex spin manifold (specifically the mixing between 3LE and 4CT states) in open-shell radical emitters is unexplored. This approach moves beyond simple steric stabilization of the radical to actively engineer its excited-state spin physics.

proposals.title: Direct Measurement of Spin-Polarized Currents in Chiral Organic Light-Emitting Diodes via the Chiral-Induced Spin Selectivity Effect
proposals.motivation: The Friend group's recent work has established that organic radical semiconductors exhibit long-lived quartet states and intrinsic intermolecular charge separation, offering a novel route to manipulate spin in organic electronics. However, a direct experimental link between the chiral molecular structure of an OLED's emissive layer and the spin polarization of its charge transport current remains unexplored. Establishing this link is crucial for developing next-generation spintronic devices that control spin without ferromagnetic contacts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chiral organic semiconductors, when incorporated as the emissive layer in an OLED architecture, will generate a spin-polarized electrical current due to the Chiral-Induced Spin Selectivity (CISS) effect, and that the degree of spin polarization will directly correlate with the device's electroluminescence circular polarization and the efficiency of triplet harvesting mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will fabricate OLEDs using chiral derivatives of high-performance thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) hosts or luminescent radical emitters, as recently developed in the group. Spin-polarized currents will be measured using a ferromagnetic tunnel junction contact in a vertical device geometry, where one electrode is a ferromagnetic Co layer and the other is a non-magnetic contact. We will correlate the measured magnetoresistance (a direct probe of spin polarization) with the device's external quantum efficiency, transient electroluminescence to track triplet dynamics, and spectrally resolved circularly polarized electroluminescence (CP-EL).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct quantification of spin currents in operational chiral OLEDs, experimentally validating the CISS effect in a working organic optoelectronic device. It will establish a foundational framework for linking spin-dependent charge transport with optical emission properties, guiding the design of spin-OLEDs for display and quantum information applications.
proposals.novelty: While CISS has been studied in molecular junctions and thin films, its application to measure spin currents in a fully functional, light-emitting diode based on state-of-the-art chiral TADF or radical emitters is unprecedented. This merges the group's expertise in organic spin states with the emerging field of chiral opto-spintronics.

proposals.title: Decoupling π-Stacking from Spin Dynamics: Designing Phenyl-Free Radical Semiconductors for Intrinsic Intermolecular Charge Separation
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has shown that organic radical semiconductors with long-lived doublet and quartet states can exhibit intrinsic, ultrafast intermolecular photoinduced charge separation, a promising property for photovoltaics and spintronics. A prevailing empirical observation is that strong intermolecular interactions, and thus efficient charge separation, require terminal phenyl groups to facilitate π-stacking. This structural constraint limits the chemical diversity and electronic tunability of radical materials. This project aims to systematically challenge and understand this requirement to unlock new design principles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the role of phenyl groups is not primarily electronic but steric and conformational, providing a rigid scaffold for optimal orbital overlap. Therefore, alternative non-aromatic, sterically rigid end-groups can be designed to facilitate equally strong intermolecular interactions and efficient charge separation without traditional π-stacking motifs.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl (TTM) radical derivatives, systematically replacing the peripheral phenyl rings with sterically rigid, non-aromatic groups (e.g., adamantyl, bicyclo[2.2.2]octyl) and π-conjugated but non-benzenoid linkers (e.g., ethynyl, fluorenyl). The materials will be characterized by transient absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy to measure charge separation yields and quartet state lifetimes. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT calculations will model intermolecular dimer geometries, electronic coupling (transfer integrals), and spin-density distributions to correlate structure with interaction strength and spin dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear molecular design rules for intermolecular interactions in radical semiconductors, moving beyond the phenyl-group paradigm. It will directly test the relationship between molecular geometry, electronic coupling, and spin-dependent charge transfer, contributing fundamental knowledge to the fields of organic spintronics and radical-based photophysics. A successful outcome would expand the synthetic toolbox for high-performance radical materials.
proposals.novelty: While the necessity of phenyl groups is an accepted heuristic, a deliberate campaign to replace them with non-aromatic alternatives to probe the fundamental mechanism of radical interaction has not been reported. The explicit focus on decoupling steric from aromatic contributions in the context of quartet-state photophysics is a novel approach.

proposals.title: Harnessing Host-to-Radical Energy Transfer in Blend Films for Enhanced Photogeneration of Long-Lived Quartet States
proposals.motivation: Organic radical semiconductors show immense promise for optoelectronics due to their spin-allowed doublet-doublet transitions and long-lived quartet states, enabling efficient photogeneration and charge separation. However, the intrinsic photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of radical emitters are often limited by non-radiative decay pathways. A strategy to enhance photogeneration efficiency without chemically modifying the radical itself is critically needed to advance devices like radical-based OLEDs and photodetectors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the energy landscape in a host-radical blend film, we can achieve efficient Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from a high-photoluminescence host matrix to the radical guest, thereby significantly boosting the population of its photoexcited states, particularly the long-lived quartet, for enhanced photogeneration yields.
proposals.method: We will fabricate thin-film blends using a series of high-triplet-energy host materials (e.g., carbazole derivatives, TADF hosts) with the model radical (4-N-carbazolyl-2,6-dichlorophenyl)bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl (TTM-1Cz). We will systematically vary the host-guest ratio and use steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy to quantify FRET efficiency and quartet state population dynamics. Transient absorption spectroscopy will be employed to directly monitor the formation and decay of the radical's quartet state following host excitation, distinguishing direct host-to-radical energy transfer from charge transfer processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear design rules for host materials in radical-based blend films, moving beyond simple doping to active energy donor roles. It will provide a novel materials strategy to decouple the absorption cross-section from the radical's intrinsic properties, potentially leading to blend films with photogeneration efficiencies surpassing those of the neat radical semiconductor.
proposals.novelty: While host engineering is mature for singlet and triplet exciton management in conventional OLEDs, its deliberate application to control the population of doublet and quartet states in organic radicals via FRET is unexplored. This approach is distinct from the intrinsic intermolecular charge separation recently studied in neat radical films.

proposals.title: Direct Magnetic Resonance Probing of Spin-State Dynamics in Photogenerated Radical Ion Pairs for Organic Optoelectronics
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that photoinduced charge separation in organic radical semiconductors creates long-lived, spin-correlated anion-cation pairs. The spin multiplicity of these pairs (singlet vs. triplet vs. quartet) fundamentally dictates their recombination pathways, charge transport, and luminescence properties, yet direct, time-resolved characterization of these spin states in operational materials is lacking. Understanding this spin physics is crucial for designing next-generation organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) and photovoltaic devices that exploit radical and multi-exciton states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pulsed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) spectroscopy will directly reveal the formation, mixing, and decay dynamics of singlet, triplet, and quartet spin manifolds within photogenerated radical pairs, and that external magnetic fields can be used to control their interconversion and resultant device performance.
proposals.method: We will apply pulsed EPR techniques, specifically electron spin echo envelope modulation (ESEEM) and hyperfine sublevel correlation spectroscopy (HYSCORE), to map the local spin environment and quantify exchange interactions in thin films of model radical semiconductors (e.g., TTM-3PCz and related donors/acceptors). Time-resolved ODMR, synchronized with pulsed laser excitation, will be used to correlate spin-state populations with photoluminescence or electroluminescence intensity in operational device stacks. Experiments will be conducted across a range of temperatures (4-300 K) and under tunable external magnetic fields (0-1 T).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct spectroscopic fingerprint of the quartet state and its coupling to singlet and triplet channels in photo-generated charge-separated pairs. It will establish a quantitative framework linking spin-state dynamics to key optoelectronic metrics, such as luminescence quantum yield and charge carrier lifetime, thereby offering a new design principle for radical-based organic electronics.
proposals.novelty: While EPR is standard for studying radicals, its application to directly probe the *interacting spin states of photogenerated anion-cation pairs* in thin-film organic semiconductors, and to correlate these states with optical function via ODMR in device-relevant architectures, is a novel and powerful approach beyond conventional photophysical characterization.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Photodetection via Intrinsic Charge Separation in Isolated Luminescent Radicals
proposals.motivation: Organic radical semiconductors exhibit unique spin-allowed optical transitions and intrinsic intermolecular photoinduced charge separation, offering a novel pathway for photodetection. However, device physics has been studied only in bulk films, obscuring the fundamental limits and mechanisms of photoresponse at the molecular scale. Creating single-molecule radical photodetectors would directly probe the quantum efficiency of charge generation from doublet and quartet states, free from intermolecular disorder.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that isolated, stable luminescent radical molecules or covalently linked radical dimers, when integrated into a nanoscale junction, will function as efficient photodetectors due to their intrinsic ability to generate long-lived charge-separated states directly upon photoexcitation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and isolate stable, luminescent radical molecules (e.g., tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl-based derivatives) and their dimers. These will be integrated into single-molecule junctions using scanning tunneling microscope (STM) break-junction techniques with optical excitation. Photocurrent will be measured as a function of wavelength, bias, and magnetic field to correlate photoresponse with the radical's specific spin states (doublet ground state, excited doublet, and quartet). Time-resolved electrical measurements will probe the lifetime of the photogenerated charge-separated state.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first proof-of-concept for a radical-based single-molecule photodetector, quantifying the quantum yield of charge separation from a defined molecular spin system. It will provide fundamental insights into how Coulombic interactions and spin multiplicity govern charge transfer efficiency at the ultimate limit of miniaturization, informing the design of future quantum optoelectronic devices.
proposals.novelty: While radical photophysics in films is known, the concept of a photodetector using a *single* radical molecule is unexplored. The approach uniquely leverages the radical's intrinsic photoinduced charge separation—a property distinct from conventional singlet exciton-based materials—to bypass exciton diffusion and dissociation steps.

proposals.title: Radical-Exciton Bilayer Diodes: A Light-Emitting Charge Injection Contact for Enhanced OLED Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Injection losses at contacts remain a significant efficiency bottleneck in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs). Pre-2026 work by Friend and others has demonstrated that radical semiconductors exhibit efficient, intrinsic intermolecular photoinduced charge separation, generating free charges with near-unity quantum efficiency. This presents a unique opportunity to reimagine the electrode interface itself as an active, light-emitting charge generation layer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a thin film of a luminescent radical semiconductor, such as (4-N-carbazolyl-2,6-dichlorophenyl)bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl (TTM-1Cz), can function as a highly efficient, optically transparent charge injector when placed between a conventional electrode and a standard fluorescent or phosphorescent emitter layer, thereby reducing the operating voltage and improving device efficiency.
proposals.method: We will fabricate bilayer devices with the architecture: ITO / radical layer (e.g., TTM-1Cz) / emissive layer (e.g., a blue fluorescent or green phosphorescent material) / cathode. The radical layer thickness will be optimized (5-50 nm) for both charge generation and optical outcoupling. We will characterize devices using current density-voltage-luminance (J-V-L) measurements, external quantum efficiency (EQE), and electroluminescence (EL) spectroscopy to quantify injection efficiency and spectral output. Transient EL and impedance spectroscopy will be used to probe charge dynamics at the radical/emitter interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new device paradigm: a 'light-emitting charge injector.' It will provide a fundamental understanding of charge transfer from doublet radical states to singlet/triplet exciton manifolds in adjacent layers. Success would demonstrate a tangible route to lower-voltage, higher-efficiency OLEDs by decoupling injection from the work function of the electrode.
proposals.novelty: While radical films have been studied as emitters or charge generation layers in photodetectors, their use as an engineered, light-emitting functional contact specifically designed to inject charges into a separate excitonic semiconductor is a novel and non-obvious application of their unique photophysics.

proposals.title: Chiral Spin-Polarized Light-Emitting Diodes Based on Radical-Triplet Hybrid States
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that organic radical semiconductors can support long-lived quartet states and efficient intermolecular charge separation, offering a new route to manipulate spin and charge. Integrating such chiral, spin-active molecules into a solid-state device could unlock novel functionalities, such as electrically generated, spin-polarized chiral light emission, which is critical for emerging technologies in quantum information and 3D displays.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a chiral luminescent radical, exhibiting enhanced fluorescence and chiral phosphorescence from its quartet manifold, can be integrated into an OLED architecture to create a proof-of-concept device that simultaneously demonstrates efficient electroluminescence and spin-filtering capabilities.
proposals.method: We will fabricate OLED devices with the target chiral radical as the primary emissive layer. Device architecture will be optimized using pre-2026 host-guest and matrix-free hyperfluorescent design principles to manage exciton dynamics and enhance radiative decay from the doublet and quartet states. Spin-dependent charge injection will be probed using ferromagnetic electrodes (e.g., La0.7Sr0.3MnO3) in a separate spin-valve device structure to directly measure magnetoresistance and confirm spin-filtering action. Photophysical characterization (time-resolved photoluminescence, circularly polarized luminescence, electroluminescence spectra) will correlate device performance with molecular spin states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first demonstration of an OLED whose emission is intrinsically linked to the spin-polarized, chiral quartet states of an organic radical. It will establish a device platform for studying spin-dependent charge transport in radical semiconductors and advance the field towards organic spintronic light sources.
proposals.novelty: While radical-based OLEDs have been explored, the combination of chiral phosphorescence, quartet state exploitation, and direct integration into a spin-filtering device architecture to produce spin-polarized chiral light is a fundamentally new approach beyond conventional singlet-triplet exciton management.

proposals.title: Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity in Single-Molecule Radical Emitters for Spin-Polarized Luminescence
proposals.motivation: The generation and control of spin-polarized currents in organic semiconductors is crucial for spintronics and quantum information science. Pre-2026 work on chiral-induced spin selectivity (CISS) has demonstrated spin filtering in molecular films, but the fundamental mechanism and its exploitation in single-molecule, luminescent spin systems remains unexplored. This project aims to bridge the gap between CISS phenomena and the rich photophysics of organic radical emitters, which possess intrinsic doublet and quartet spin states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that helical chirality, engineered into a luminescent organic radical molecule, can generate significant spin polarization at the single-molecule level through the CISS effect, directly influencing the population of its emissive doublet and long-lived quartet states upon photoexcitation or charge injection.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of TADF-active, luminescent radical cores (e.g., based on tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl derivatives) covalently linked to chiral, helical scaffolds (e.g., helicenes). Single-molecule spectroscopy at cryogenic temperatures will be employed to probe spin-dependent photoluminescence. Spin polarization will be quantified using magnetic circular dichroism (MCD) and electrically detected magnetic resonance (EDMR) in single-molecule junctions, where the chiral molecule bridges ferromagnetic electrodes with defined magnetization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, molecule-level link between structural chirality and spin polarization in functional radical semiconductors. It will provide the first demonstration of a single-molecule spin polarizer, offering a new design principle for organic spintronic devices and potentially enabling spin control in quantum light sources without external magnetic fields.
proposals.novelty: While CISS is known in films and radical photophysics is established, the combination to achieve and measure spin polarization in a *single*, luminescent chiral radical molecule is a novel and non-obvious step. It moves beyond ensemble averaging to probe the fundamental quantum interplay of chirality, charge, and spin.

proposals.title: Spin-Controlled Circularly Polarized Luminescence from Chiral Organic Radical Emitters
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work has established that luminescent organic radicals, such as tris(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl)methyl (TTM) derivatives, exhibit unique spin properties including long-lived doublet and quartet excited states, enabling efficient intramolecular charge transfer and photoluminescence. Simultaneously, the field of circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) from chiral organic molecules is advancing for applications in 3D displays and quantum information. However, the fundamental connection between spin multiplicity (doublet vs. quartet) and the degree of CPL in a chiral radical system remains unexplored, representing a critical knowledge gap at the intersection of spintronics and chiral photonics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spin multiplicity of the emissive excited state in a helically-chiral persistent radical directly governs the magnitude and sign of its circularly polarized luminescence, with quartet states offering a distinct pathway for spin-controlled chiral light emission compared to doublet states.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of helically-chiral derivatives of known luminescent radical cores (e.g., TTM-carbazole), varying the point of chiral attachment to control the helicity. Photophysical characterization will include time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) and transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy to identify and quantify doublet and quartet state populations and lifetimes. CPL and g_lum dissymmetry factors will be measured with a spectrofluoropolarimeter. Magnetic field-dependent CPL experiments will be conducted to probe the spin-state influence on chiral emission, correlating spectroscopic data with quantum chemical calculations (TD-DFT) of excited state spin densities and chiroptical properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property relationships linking molecular chirality, radical spin state, and CPL performance, providing design rules for a new class of spin-active chiral light sources. It will advance the fundamental understanding of how exchange interactions and spin-orbit coupling in radical multiplets influence chiroptical phenomena, potentially opening avenues for CPL-based spin readout or chiral organic spin-LEDs.
proposals.novelty: While CPL from chiral closed-shell molecules and luminescence from organic radicals are both established, the deliberate merger of these concepts to study spin-dependent CPL in open-shell chiral systems is a novel and unexplored direction, moving beyond the current focus on singlet or triplet states in chiral emitters.

proposals.title: Mapping Spin-Polarization Pathways in Organic Radical Semiconductors via Non-Adiabatic Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that organic radical semiconductors exhibit long-lived quartet states and intrinsic photoinduced charge separation, offering a novel route to spin-polarized carriers for spintronics and quantum information science. However, the fundamental quantum dynamics governing the generation of spin polarization from photoexcited singlet states to doublet and quartet manifolds remain poorly understood, hindering rational material design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific molecular vibrations and conformational fluctuations govern the non-adiabatic transitions between singlet, doublet, and quartet states in luminescent radicals, and that these spin-polarization pathways can be controlled through targeted chemical modification of the radical's environment.
proposals.method: We will employ surface hopping non-adiabatic molecular dynamics (NAMD) simulations, specifically the fewest-switches surface hopping (FSSH) algorithm, on model radical systems derived from recent experimental studies. Simulations will be performed using the PyUNIxMD package or a modified version of Newton-X, with on-the-fly electronic structure calculations (TD-DFT or CASSCF) to compute spin-orbit couplings and non-adiabatic coupling vectors between relevant singlet, doublet, and quartet potential energy surfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomistic, dynamical map of spin-polarization generation in organic radicals, identifying key vibrational modes and structural motifs that promote efficient quartet formation. It will establish design principles for next-generation radical-based materials with enhanced spin properties for opto-spintronic devices.
proposals.novelty: While NAMD is commonly used for singlet exciton dynamics, its application to explicitly model spin-state interconversion between open-shell radical states (doublet-quartet) and their precursor closed-shell singlets is a novel and non-trivial extension, directly addressing the quantum spin dynamics hinted at in recent experimental studies.

proposals.title: Macroscopic Spin-Polarized Currents from Chiral Conjugated Polymers with Engineered Diradical Character
proposals.motivation: Recent work by Friend and others has demonstrated that organic radical semiconductors exhibit unique spin-dependent photophysics, such as long-lived quartet states and intrinsic photoinduced charge separation. These properties are promising for organic spintronics and quantum information science, but have been confined to small molecules or dimers. Scaling these quantum phenomena to the macroscopic, solid-state regime is a critical unmet challenge for functional device integration.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating stable diradical motifs into a chiral, conjugated polymer backbone will create a material that exhibits macroscopic spin-polarized charge transport, where the polymer's chirality acts as an effective internal magnetic field to lift spin degeneracy and maintain spin coherence over extended length scales.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of donor-acceptor conjugated copolymers where the acceptor unit is a stable, bridged triarylmethyl-type diradical, known for its high doublet-quartet energy gap. Chirality will be introduced via chiral solubilizing side chains and/or a chiral backbone conformation. Polymers will be characterized by EPR, magnetometry, and ultrafast spectroscopy to probe spin dynamics. Spin-polarized charge transport will be measured in field-effect transistor geometries using ferromagnetic electrodes with different coercivities to perform Hanle-type measurements of spin valve effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first design principles for macromolecular, solution-processable organic materials with intrinsic spin-polarizing functionality. It will directly bridge the molecular quantum properties of radical pairs with the macroscopic requirements for spin-based electronics, opening a new materials platform for organic spintronics.
proposals.novelty: While chiral-induced spin selectivity (CISS) is known in biomolecules and some polymers, combining it with intrinsic, correlated radical spins in a conjugated system is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond host-guest systems to create an intrinsically spin-active polymer where charge, spin, and chirality are engineered synergistically.

ideas.title: Engineering Topological Spin Order and Emergent Singlet Pathways in High-Spin Polyradical Frameworks
ideas.description: This research direction focuses on synthesizing and modeling extended polyradical chains, ladders, and 2D networks where the primary magnetic interaction favors high-spin (triplet or higher) ground states. The key innovation is to deliberately engineer specific sites, linker motifs, or topological defects within these networks where the local singlet configuration becomes lower in energy than the local triplet, creating embedded 'singlet wells' or pathways. This allows the investigation of emergent phenomena, such as spin-state exciton propagation, magnetic polaron formation, or the use of these singlet channels for optically addressing and manipulating spins in a high-spin quantum material. The goal is to achieve materials that combine bulk magnetic order with optically accessible, spin-selective quantum processes for applications in quantum information science and spintronics.

ideas.title: Machine Learning-Guided Discovery of High Spin-Photon Coupling States in Low-PLQY Diradical Emitters
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning with excited-state electronic structure theory to systematically explore and exploit diradical organic molecules with inherently low PLQY but strong spin-photon coupling. A model is trained on computed wavefunctions and properties (e.g., spin-orbit coupling matrix elements, transition dipole moments, exchange integrals) to automatically classify and predict new diradical excited states into categories such as 'high spin-photon interface efficiency' or 'dark-state protected qubit candidate'. This enables high-throughput virtual screening of chemical space to identify optimal materials for specific quantum applications—like spin-photon interfaces or long-lived spin qubits—where high PLQY is not only unnecessary but often detrimental. The synergy lies in using ML to decode the complex structure-property relationships that govern spin-photon coupling, turning the traditional weakness of low yield into a design principle for quantum technologies.

ideas.title: High-Temperature Coherent Control via Engineered Diradical Character
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to design and synthesize molecular systems with systematically quantifiable diradical character to serve as coherent spin qubits at elevated temperatures. By correlating the measured degree of incipient diradical character—a ground-state electronic property—with the temperature dependence of spin coherence times, we aim to identify molecular design rules that maximize the thermal resilience of quantum coherence. This enables a closed feedback loop where quantum control experiments at non-cryogenic temperatures directly inform the synthesis of next-generation molecules with optimized diradical landscapes. The ultimate goal is to establish a new class of high-temperature, chemically tunable quantum materials whose coherent properties are fundamentally encoded in their engineered electronic structure.

ideas.title: Apply chiral-induced spin selectivity (CISS)

ideas.title: Topological Design Rules for Emergent Properties in Polyradical Architectures
ideas.description: This research direction systematically maps how specific, localized structural modifications—such as linker topology, conjugation pattern, and heteroatom substitution—propagate and couple to define the global electronic structure in one-dimensional chains and two-dimensional networks of interacting radical centers. It moves beyond correlating structure with simple diradical categories to establishing design principles for complex, cooperative states like topological magnon bands, spin frustration, or macroscopic quantum coherence. The core synergy is using high-throughput computational screening and controlled synthesis of model systems to decode how local structural variables engineer long-range spin-spin interactions and delocalization, ultimately creating a blueprint for next-generation organic quantum materials and spintronic devices.

ideas.title: Solvent-Engineered Spin Baths for High-Temperature Molecular Quantum Control
ideas.description: This research direction merges the study of solvent-stabilized zwitterionic states with the goal of high-temperature coherent spin control. It proposes to engineer molecular spin qubits or candidates for quantum sensing by deliberately placing them in polar environments that stabilize specific charge-separated (zwitterionic) electronic states. This environmental control is used to tailor the spin Hamiltonian and shield the spin from local magnetic noise, effectively creating a 'solvent-engineered spin bath'. The synergy enables the demonstration of coherent microwave control at biologically or technologically relevant temperatures by using the environment as a design parameter to mitigate the primary source of thermal decoherence.

ideas.title: Investigate dynamics between diradical state categories
ideas.description: Study non-adiabatic transitions between diradical, zwitterionic, and biexciton states after photoexcitation.

ideas.title: Environmentally-Embedded DMRG for Correlated Diradicals
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the high-accuracy for strongly correlated electrons offered by Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) methods with the explicit treatment of solvent and environmental effects. The core innovation is to develop and apply an environmentally-embedded DMRG framework where key solvent molecules or a polarizable environment are treated as part of the active quantum system, directly coupled to the diradical's correlated electronic structure. This allows us to rigorously compute how environmental polarization stabilizes zwitterionic or charge-separated states, quantifying the resultant changes in diradical character, spin-spin correlations, and entanglement entropy. The approach moves beyond the limitations of gas-phase calculations and continuum dielectric models to reveal the microscopic mechanisms of solvent-induced state switching in complex diradicals and biradicaloids.

ideas.title: Mapping Conical Intersections and Competing Pathways in Tunable Singlet Fission Chromophores
ideas.description: This project investigates the non-adiabatic dynamics between diradical, zwitterionic, and multiexciton states by focusing on a tunable series of singlet fission candidate molecules with controlled diradical character. A combined computational and experimental approach is used: high-level multireference calculations (e.g., CASSCF/NEVPT2) map the multidimensional potential energy surfaces and locate key conical intersections governing the transitions. These theoretical predictions directly guide ultrafast spectroscopic experiments (2D electronic spectroscopy, femtosecond stimulated Raman) on the synthesized molecular series to observe wavepacket motion and branching ratios in real time. The systematic molecular variation allows for the derivation of clear structure-dynamics relationships, revealing how specific structural perturbations control the competition between diradical, charge-transfer, and correlated triplet-pair pathways after photoexcitation.

ideas.title: Kinetically Protected Open-Shell Ground States via Steric and Electronic Encapsulation
ideas.description: This approach designs molecules where a predesigned open-shell core is synthetically locked into a protective three-dimensional scaffold featuring bulky, rigid substituents. The scaffold's geometry and electronic structure are tuned to make the open-shell singlet or triplet the unambiguous ground state, moving beyond simple diradicals. Crucially, the encapsulating structure provides a kinetic barrier to decomposition, enabling the isolation, characterization, and potential processing of these molecules as stable materials for spintronics or nonlinear optics.

ideas.title: Defining the Isolated Diradical Baseline in Rigid Matrices
ideas.description: This study aims to establish the fundamental spectroscopic and magnetic signatures of molecular diradicals in a state of complete isolation, free from intermolecular interactions. Diradicals are doped at ultralow concentrations into inert, rigid crystalline or glassy matrices and studied at cryogenic temperatures to freeze molecular motion. Using a suite of techniques including high-frequency EPR, SQUID magnetometry, and optical spectroscopy, the intrinsic properties of the isolated diradical—such as its singlet-triplet energy gap, zero-field splitting parameters, and electronic structure—are measured with high precision. This provides the essential baseline required to later understand and quantify how these properties change upon deliberate introduction of intermolecular interactions in films or aggregates.

ideas.title: Investigate blend films with radical-host energy transfer

ideas.title: Closed-loop device optimization using ML-classified diradical states
ideas.description: This research direction integrates a luminescent diradical material into a solid-state device equipped with real-time spectroscopic readout. A machine learning model, pre-trained on computed wavefunctions, continuously classifies the diradical's excited state from the device's optical output. This classification is then fed into a control system that dynamically adjusts device parameters—such as applied field, cavity geometry, or excitation pulse—to stabilize a desired diradical state or transition. This closed-loop integration enables autonomous, adaptive devices that optimize their own performance for specific quantum sensing tasks like magnetometry or spin-photon interfacing, based on the identified molecular quantum state.

ideas.title: Pulsed EPR Interrogation of Diradical/Zwitterionic Defect States for Quantum Material Engineering
ideas.description: This research direction combines the valence-bond theory framework for analyzing solid-state defects with advanced pulsed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. It aims to systematically classify paramagnetic point defects or edge states in semiconductors and 2D materials as having dominant diradical (correlated, open-shell) or zwitterionic (ionic, closed-shell) character based on their measured spin Hamiltonian parameters. Pulsed techniques like HYSCORE and ESEEM will quantify hyperfine and quadrupole interactions, which are direct signatures of local spin density and polarity, while coherence time (T2) measurements will reveal how the electronic structure impacts spin dephasing. This synergy provides a predictive materials design loop: from theoretical electronic structure categories to experimentally validated spin qubit candidates with tailored coherence properties.

ideas.title: Predictive Mapping of Triplet Diradical Manifolds: From Classification to Functional Property Design
ideas.description: This project moves beyond a comparative classification to build a predictive, quantitative framework linking the structural and electronic features of triplet diradicals to their functional behavior. By integrating quantum chemical descriptors with the classification scheme, we construct a structure-property map that identifies the physical drivers of spin-state stability and coupling. The map's predictive power is then rigorously tested by designing novel triplet diradical candidates with targeted properties, such as enhanced spin-switching ability or specific magnetic interactions, and validating their predicted behavior via high-level computational analysis. This approach transforms a descriptive taxonomy into a tool for the rational design of triplet diradicals for spintronics or catalysis.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Diradical Optical Gaps via Quantum Control Analogies
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem framework from quantum coherent control to the domain of diradical design. In quantum control, a target quantum outcome is specified, and an optimal control field is computed inversely. Here, the target is a specific excited-state energy (optical gap), and the system is a diradical's electronic state mixture. The analogous solution is to treat the diradical's chemical building blocks—bridging units, substituents, and overall topology—as the adjustable 'control parameters.' An inverse computational algorithm (e.g., machine learning optimization or iterative quantum chemistry calculations) would be employed to search the space of possible molecular structures, identifying those whose calculated state mixtures produce the desired optical gap. This transforms molecular design from a forward, trial-and-error process into a targeted inverse engineering problem, where the molecule is 'shaped' to meet a precise photophysical specification.

ideas.title: Mapping the Emergent Diradical-to-Polyradical Continuum in Extended Systems
ideas.description: This research direction unifies the precise quantification of incipient diradical character in nominally closed-shell units with the study of extended polyradical systems. The core objective is to treat localized diradicaloid character not as a binary property but as a quantifiable 'seed' state, and systematically investigate how these seeds interact, couple, and potentially delocalize when integrated into one-dimensional chains and two- or three-dimensional networks. This creates a novel continuum perspective, connecting the closed-shell limit to the high-spin polyradical limit through a controlled framework. The synergy allows for the bottom-up design of organic magnetic, conductive, or responsive materials by engineering the type, strength, and topology of interactions between pre-characterized diradicaloid sites.

ideas.title: Applying Solid-State Doping Strategies to Control Diradical Interactions in Aggregates
ideas.description: The original assumption that molecular diradicals must be isolated in solution to be studied is challenged by proposing to study them in concentrated, interacting states. Drawing an analogy from semiconductor physics, where doping is used to controllably alter electronic interactions in a solid-state lattice, this idea adapts the doping strategy for diradical systems. The research would involve creating composite materials—such as concentrated solutions, thin films, or covalent networks—where active diradical molecules are 'doped' with inert, diamagnetic matrix molecules. These spacer molecules act as tunable impurities, physically separating diradical centers and modulating their magnetic exchange interactions (J). This approach allows for the systematic deconvolution of how specific intermolecular contacts (e.g., π-stacking, van der Waals) influence diradical character, spin-spin coupling, and ultimately bulk properties like magnetic susceptibility or conductivity, moving beyond the isolated-molecule paradigm.

ideas.title: In-situ generation of stable diradical polaritons via matrix isolation in optical microcavities
ideas.description: This redesigned approach integrates the synthesis of the reactive diradical directly within the photonic cavity to ensure stability and strong coupling. A stable precursor molecule is embedded in a solid argon or neon matrix deposited on a cavity mirror under cryogenic, ultra-high vacuum conditions. The active diradical is then generated in situ via a precise light or heat pulse, creating the target open-shell species already positioned within the cavity mode volume. This method bypasses diradical handling and degradation, enabling the study of strong coupling and polariton formation with intrinsically unstable but highly spin-active molecular quantum materials.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop ML-Driven Design of High-Exchange-Energy Diradicals via Excited-State Feedback
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning-based state classification directly into the molecular optimization pipeline for diradicals. An ML model, trained on computed wavefunctions, automatically analyzes and categorizes the excited-state landscape of candidate diradicals generated during the search for larger ground-state exchange energy (J). The key synergy is using the ML-predicted excited-state character—such as the presence of low-lying triplet or charge-transfer states that could compromise stability—as a critical feedback signal to guide the design algorithm. This enables the simultaneous optimization for both a large, thermally robust J and a benign excited-state profile, a multi-objective goal neither approach could achieve independently.

ideas.description: Design molecules where the open-shell form is the ground state, not accessed by twisting.

ideas.title: Use molecular self-assembly to pattern diradicals in optical cavities
ideas.description: The original idea proposes placing diradicals in photonic cavities for strong coupling. Drawing an analogy from supramolecular chemistry and surface science, where self-assembly techniques (like SAMs) are used to create ordered molecular monolayers on substrates for electronics, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to use molecular self-assembly to pattern diradicals in optical cavities. By functionalizing diradical molecules with specific anchor groups, they can be made to spontaneously form a dense, uniform monolayer directly on the cavity mirror surfaces at the position of maximum field strength. This bottom-up assembly method provides superior spatial and orientational control over the diradical emitters compared to simple deposition, thereby maximizing the collective coupling strength and enabling more efficient and controlled polariton formation for studying and manipulating diradical quantum states.

ideas.title: Sacrificing Brightness for Coherence: A Quantum Transducer Design Paradigm from Radio Astronomy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is universally necessary for effective spin-photon interfaces. Drawing an analogy from radio astronomy, where the priority is amplifying faint signals with minimal added noise rather than maximizing raw power, we propose a shift in metrics for quantum materials. The analogous solution involved designing cryogenic low-noise amplifiers (e.g., HEMTs, masers) that prioritize signal integrity and SNR. Adapted to quantum photonics, the research would explore and engineer solid-state spin systems (like specific defect centers or quantum dots in tailored cavities) where a lower PLQY is accepted in exchange for dramatically stronger spin-photon coupling or higher spin-photon entanglement fidelity. The goal is to optimize for the quantum cooperativity and interface fidelity—the quantum equivalents of SNR—rather than brightness alone, making these systems superior for quantum networking and coherent sensing applications where the quality of the quantum link is more critical than the photon count.

ideas.title: Quantitative Mapping of Zwitterionic Stabilization: From Intrinsic Molecular Dipole to Environmental Polarity
ideas.description: This project moves beyond qualitative demonstration to establish a predictive, quantitative framework for how environments control zwitterionic character. A homologous series of push-pull molecules, designed to span a continuous range of intrinsic dipole moment changes between canonical and zwitterionic limits, will be studied in a hierarchy of environments: from inert gas matrices and non-polar solvents, through a graded series of polar solvents, to ordered polar interfaces and synthetic supramolecular cages. Using a combination of steady-state and ultrafast spectroscopy to track electronic structure, coupled with advanced electrostatic embedding calculations, the work will generate a two-dimensional map plotting the degree of zwitterionic character against both the molecular dipole moment change and a rigorously defined environmental polarity parameter. This map will provide a generalizable rule for predicting state hybridization in complex, condensed-phase systems.

ideas.title: Spin Bath Decoupling via Dynamic Symmetry for High-Temperature Coherence
ideas.description: This idea proposes to extend coherent microwave control of spins to higher temperatures by drawing an analogy from structural biology, where nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) of proteins achieves high-resolution coherence at room temperature. The analogous solution involves isotopic labeling to remove noisy spin nuclei and advanced pulse sequences to decouple the system from its environment. Adapted to solid-state spin qubits, the research would focus on synthesizing ultra-pure host materials (e.g., isotopically engineered diamond) to reduce the intrinsic spin bath, combined with the development of novel microwave control sequences that are specifically designed to suppress decoherence pathways dominant at elevated temperatures, such as phonon-induced relaxation and spectral diffusion from thermally activated defects. The goal is to create a dynamically protected spin state whose coherence is less sensitive to thermal agitation.

ideas.title: Adapting Statistical Mechanics of Spin Glasses to Polyradical Networks
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from condensed matter physics. The analogous problem is understanding complex, disordered magnetic systems known as spin glasses, where many interacting spins lead to a rugged energy landscape and novel phases. That problem was solved using replica theory, numerical simulations on finite lattices, and the identification of appropriate order parameters. Adapted to the context of polyradical molecules, the idea is to treat molecules with multiple radical centers as quantum spin lattices. The research would employ advanced computational methods (like DMRG for 1D chains and tensor networks for 2D networks) to simulate these systems, mapping how their collective electronic states—such as multiradical singlets or magnetically ordered domains—emerge and interact based on molecular architecture and the strength of exchange couplings between radical sites. This approach provides a framework to categorize and predict the behavior of complex polyradical materials.

ideas.title: Cavity-Engineered Quantum Materials via Defect Polaritons
ideas.description: This research direction integrates solid-state defects, such as vacancies or edge states in 2D materials, into high-finesse optical microcavities to achieve strong light-matter coupling. The key innovation is using the cavity vacuum field to hybridize the localized defect states, which are described by a valence-bond/diradical framework, into polaritonic modes. This allows for the real-time, non-chemical manipulation of the defect's spin multiplicity, exchange coupling, and chemical reactivity via optical tuning of the cavity resonance. The synergy enables the creation of 'cavity quantum materials' where photonic dressing provides a new knob to engineer quantum phases and defect functionalities not accessible in bare materials.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Force Field Parameterization for Diradical Parameter Prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biochemistry, where high-level QM calculations on small fragments are used to derive force field parameters for simulating large proteins. The analogous problem in quantum materials design is predicting Hubbard U and exchange energies for new, complex diradicals, which is computationally prohibitive at a high level of theory for the full system. The adapted solution is to perform high-level quantum chemistry calculations (e.g., CASSCF, DMRG, or high-level DFT) not on the entire target diradical, but on strategically chosen, smaller molecular fragments that embody its critical magnetic sites and their local environments. From these fragment calculations, one can extract ab initio-derived parameters for electron correlation (U) and magnetic exchange (J). These predicted parameters are then used to parameterize an effective model Hamiltonian (like a Heisenberg or Hubbard model) for the full diradical, enabling efficient screening and predictive design of diradicals with desired magnetic properties (e.g., strong ferromagnetic coupling or specific spin ground states) before synthesis.

ideas.title: Topology-Adapted Tensor Network Methods for Large Correlated Diradical Systems
ideas.description: This research develops and applies tensor network methods whose geometry is explicitly adapted to the non-linear, often two-dimensional, connectivity of large diradical molecules. Instead of forcing the system onto a one-dimensional DMRG chain, the approach maps the molecular graph onto an optimized tree or hybrid network topology, dramatically improving the efficiency of capturing long-range electronic correlations. The framework automates the construction and variational optimization of these tailored ansatzes for quantum chemical Hamiltonians. This enables the accurate study of open-shell character, singlet-triplet gaps, and correlation effects in previously intractable polycyclic diradicals and nanographene structures.

ideas.title: Robust High-Contrast Spin-Photon Interface via Dynamic Spectral Alignment and Stability-Optimized Control
ideas.description: This project moves beyond chasing a single high-contrast value by designing a material-experimental control loop that ensures high optical readout contrast is maintained under realistic operating conditions. The core innovation is a hybrid diamond-silicon nitride photonic platform where the optical resonance can be tuned via strain or temperature to actively align with the emitter's spectrum, countering drift. A real-time analysis algorithm deconvolves the photoluminescence signal to isolate the contrast contribution, and a feedback controller adjusts microwave parameters and cavity alignment to maximize contrast over a multi-dimensional stability space, inherently producing a more reliable and useful quantum interface.

ideas.title: Quantifying Incipient Diradical Character via a Quantum Phase Transition Analogy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the framework of quantum critical phenomena from condensed matter physics to systematically quantify incipient diradical states in nearly closed-shell molecules. In the analogous field, the behavior of a system near a quantum phase transition is characterized by universal scaling laws and critical exponents, providing a continuous measure of how 'close' the system is to a new phase. The adapted solution is to conceptualize the emergence of diradical character as a molecular-scale quantum critical point. By identifying a suitable molecular control parameter (such as bond alternation, torsional angle, or applied external perturbation) and analyzing how quantum chemical descriptors (e.g., <S^2>, natural orbital occupancies, or singlet-triplet gap) scale with this parameter, one can derive a quantitative, scalable metric for diradical character. This approach moves beyond binary classification, offering a dimensional scaling framework (SCALING_DUAL) that ranks and compares molecules based on their 'criticality' and the scaling behavior of their electronic properties near the closed-shell/diradical boundary.

ideas.title: Predictive Diradical Descriptor Maps for Functional Molecular Design
ideas.description: This research moves beyond static classification by establishing a causal, predictive link between quantified incipient diradical character and key molecular properties. A computational pipeline first systematically calculates a robust diradical index for a vast library of nearly closed-shell molecules. These indices are then used as core descriptors to train machine learning models on a suite of target properties, including optical gaps, singlet-triplet energy splittings, and specific reaction energetics. The resulting predictive maps enable the inverse design of molecules with tailored diradical character to achieve desired functional performance in areas like organic electronics, photochemistry, and catalysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Solid-State Physics' Dielectric Screening to Map Solvent-Stabilized Molecular States
ideas.description: This research challenges the gas-phase assumption in computational chemistry by quantitatively adapting the dielectric screening formalism from solid-state physics. In semiconductors, the dielectric constant of the host material screens Coulomb interactions, drastically stabilizing charge-separated impurity states. The analogous solution is to treat molecular zwitterions as 'impurities' within a solvent 'host.' The adapted idea develops a computational methodology where the solvent environment is parameterized by its frequency-dependent dielectric function to construct a screened Hamiltonian for the solute. This framework will map how the energetic ordering and character (zwitterionic vs. canonical) of molecular states depend explicitly on the solvent's polarizability and relaxation dynamics, moving beyond implicit continuum models to a more physically grounded description of environmental stabilization.

ideas.title: Use ultrafast X-ray spectroscopy to track exciton dynamics with site-specific resolution
ideas.description: The original idea uses ultrafast optical spectroscopy to track exciton dynamics. Drawing an analogy from structural biology, where time-resolved X-ray techniques capture atomic-scale, site-specific snapshots of fast enzymatic reactions, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to employ ultrafast X-ray spectroscopy (e.g., femtosecond XANES) at a free-electron laser source to track exciton dynamics in photovoltaic or photocatalytic materials. This method would provide element-specific and site-selective resolution of intersystem crossing and charge separation pathways, revealing the role of specific atomic sites in these ultrafast processes, which is information largely hidden to purely optical probes.

ideas.title: Enhance Photoluminescence Contrast via Acoustic Cavity Coupling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT), where a low-coherence interferometer selectively amplifies signals from a specific depth by path-length matching, drastically improving contrast. The analogous problem of improving photoluminescence contrast at microwave resonance is addressed by designing a material or heterostructure that incorporates a tailored acoustic or microwave cavity around the optical spin defect (e.g., a diamond NV center or silicon vacancy). This cavity is engineered to be resonant with the target microwave frequency used for spin manipulation. It functions as a selective amplifier, concentrating the microwave field at the defect site and minimizing off-resonant excitation of the bulk or surrounding material. This targeted enhancement should lead to a stronger modulation of the defect's spin-dependent photoluminescence, pushing the optical read-out contrast beyond the 8% benchmark by improving the signal-to-background ratio, analogous to how OCT isolates a signal from a specific tissue layer.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Energy Landscape Analysis to Solid-State Defect States
ideas.description: This idea proposes to transfer the conceptual and computational framework of energy landscape analysis, well-established in protein folding studies, to the solid-state problem of defect and edge state electronic structure. In protein folding, the energy landscape theory models the stability and pathways between conformational states. Analogously, for a material defect (like a vacancy or dopant) or a graphene nanoribbon edge, one can construct a multi-dimensional energy landscape where the axes are relevant local structural distortions and electronic order parameters (e.g., diradical character, charge separation). The minima on this landscape correspond to stable electronic configurations (diradical, zwitterionic, etc.), while saddle points represent transition states. By applying adapted computational sampling and analysis techniques from biochemistry, such as metadynamics or kinetic network models, to solid-state simulations (DFT, model Hamiltonians), one can systematically analyze the stability, accessibility, and interconversion of these defect electronic states. This provides a powerful method to move beyond static classification of defect states to a dynamic, pathway-informed understanding of their electronic behavior under non-equilibrium conditions.

ideas.title: Environmentally Screened Magnetic Parameter Prediction via Integrated First-Principles and Machine Learning
ideas.description: This approach combines high-level quantum chemistry calculations with a data-driven correction framework to predict accurate, environmentally screened magnetic parameters (Hubbard U and exchange J) for new diradicals. The system first computes unscreened values using ab initio methods, then refines them using a machine learning model trained on a benchmark set of experimental data to account for dynamic screening and solid-state/matrix effects. This hybrid strategy directly addresses the systematic error of purely theoretical predictions, ensuring the output parameters are relevant for real-world material behavior. The result is a robust, predictive design tool with validated accuracy for guiding the synthesis of diradicals with targeted magnetic properties.

The analogous problem of disentangling overlapping hyperfine interactions and assigning spins to specific nuclei in EPR is solved by transferring the NMR methodology. The adapted research idea is to develop pulsed EPR experiments that create two-dimensional correlation spectra, for instance correlating electron spin echo envelope modulation (ESEEM) frequencies with hyperfine dimensions. This would allow for the unambiguous assignment of hyperfine couplings to specific nuclei, dramatically improving the detailed mapping of spin density and local environment for complex spin systems like metalloproteins or quantum bits.
