ideas.title: Topological classification of triplet diradical manifolds using methods from condensed matter physics
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from condensed matter physics to the original problem of classifying triplet diradical states. In topological insulators, the manifold of electronic wavefunctions is classified by topological invariants that capture global, non-local properties beyond local energy comparisons. We adapt this by mapping the manifold of triplet diradical electronic states, defined by variables like molecular conformation and external perturbations, to a parameter space. By computing appropriate topological invariants (e.g., Berry phases or related quantities) for this manifold, we can establish a rigorous classification scheme that groups triplet diradicals into distinct topological classes, revealing connections and discontinuities not apparent from local energy considerations alone.

ideas.title: A Robust Protocol for Quantifying Genuine Quantum Correlation in Diradicals via Particle Fluctuations and Entanglement Benchmarks
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, multi-step protocol to quantify the genuine quantum mechanical character of diradical states, moving beyond the direct application of entanglement measures. First, it defines a physically observable metric based on particle number fluctuations in localized active orbitals, which is intrinsic to the molecule and independent of computational method details. This core metric is then rigorously benchmarked across a hierarchy of electronic structure theories to establish error bounds and identify method-independent trends. Finally, a calibrated mapping is constructed to translate this robust physical quantity into quantum information theoretic measures, clearly delineating the contribution of true quantum entanglement from total electron correlation and ensuring the results are predictive and transferable.

ideas.title: Quantify Diradical Character via Network Theory Metrics for Quantum Spin Systems
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from network science, where the structure of complex systems is quantified using graph-theoretic metrics like centrality and modularity. The analogous problem—mapping hidden functional modules from interaction data—is solved by constructing a network of components and analyzing its topological features. Adapted to quantum chemistry, we propose mapping the electronic wavefunction's spin-orbital correlations into a weighted network, where nodes are orbitals and edge weights are pairwise quantum entanglement or correlation measures (e.g., from a two-orbital entropy analysis). By then applying network metrics—such as the von Neumann entropy of the network's reduced correlation matrix or community detection algorithms—we can identify clusters of strongly correlated orbitals that localize unpaired electrons. This provides a spatially-resolved, multi-orbital quantification of diradical character, capturing its delocalized nature in complex molecules more effectively than traditional two-orbital indices.

ideas.title: Adapt Tensor-Train Decomposition from Machine Learning for Efficient DMRG in Large Diradical Systems
ideas.description: The original idea proposes transferring DMRG methods from condensed matter physics to study larger, correlated diradical systems in quantum chemistry. By drawing an analogy to the field of Machine Learning and High-Dimensional Data Compression, specifically the solution of tensor-train decomposition for compressing complex data, we can adapt this approach. The analogous solution uses a chain of low-rank tensors to represent high-dimensional data efficiently. Adapting this, the research idea is to integrate the tensor-train decomposition framework into the DMRG algorithm to compress the wavefunction tensors for large diradical systems. This reformulation would allow DMRG calculations to handle significantly larger active spaces and more complex electron correlations with manageable computational resources, advancing the study of diradical character and biradicaloids in molecular design.

ideas.title: Chemical Descriptor Heuristics for Rapid State Characterization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that multireference quantum chemistry methods must be the primary or initial analysis tool for characterizing electronic states. By drawing an analogy from drug discovery—where simple molecular descriptors filter vast compound libraries—we propose developing a suite of simple, approximate chemical descriptors for rapid state categorization. These descriptors, calculable from low-cost quantum methods or molecular structure alone (e.g., orbital gaps, symmetry indices, local spin indicators), would be used to create heuristic rules or a lightweight classifier. This tool would predict state categories (e.g., single-reference, multireference, diradicaloid) quickly and transparently, acting as a pre-screening filter to guide and reduce reliance on computationally heavy multireference calculations, thereby streamlining computational workflow and resource allocation.

ideas.title: Active Phonon Engineering and Bath Stabilization for High-Temperature Coherent Control of Scalable Spin Qubits
ideas.description: This project aims to demonstrate coherent control of spin qubits in silicon at temperatures above 50 K by directly attacking the phonon-induced decoherence bottleneck. The approach combines custom microwave pulse sequences designed to decouple spins from a thermally agitated phonon bath with real-time feedback control of a pulsed heating stage to maintain a stable spin environment during manipulation. Success will be measured by the preservation of gate fidelity and coherence time (T2) as temperature is increased, moving beyond proof-of-principle in exotic systems to a critical path for scalable, warmer-operation quantum processors.

ideas.title: Causal Graph Discovery for Diradical Character and Photophysical Properties
ideas.description: This project moves beyond correlation to establish causal structure-property relationships in diradical systems. It combines automated quantum chemical calculations with causal discovery algorithms to model how discrete structural perturbations directly influence diradical configurations and subsequent optical properties. The framework generates testable causal hypotheses, prioritizing synthetic targets most likely to validate or refute proposed mechanistic pathways. This creates a predictive, explanatory model that identifies which structural features are genuine levers for property control, robust to hidden confounders.

ideas.title: Apply a phylogenetic classification framework to diverse diradical molecules
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology to test the generality of a diradical classification protocol. In systematics, a standardized cladistic analysis is applied to diverse organisms to map traits and define groups based on shared derived characteristics. By analogizing this approach, we will treat a set of quantum chemical descriptors as classification traits and apply the protocol to a phylogenetically inspired, diverse set of diradical systems (spanning different structural motifs and compositions). This will identify robust classification categories and the key electronic-structure descriptors that define them, rigorously testing the protocol's applicability beyond the original pQDM system.

ideas.title: Adapting Image Recognition for Wavefunction-Based Diradical State Classification
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computer vision and medical imaging, where convolutional neural networks (CNNs) classify complex patterns in 2D/3D image data for diagnosis. The analogous problem is the need to categorize intricate, high-dimensional patterns—here, in medical scans, and in the original idea, in computed quantum wavefunctions. The adapted solution is to treat wavefunction data (e.g., electron density plots, natural orbital grids, or matrix representations) as input 'images' for a CNN. The model would be trained on a labeled dataset of wavefunctions for known diradical state types. By learning the spatial correlations and features indicative of specific diradical characters (like spin density distributions or orbital nodal patterns), the CNN can then automatically categorize new, computed excited states, providing a robust and automated alternative to manual analysis based on heuristic quantum chemical indices.
